target.title: [codex] Suppress empty native reasoning placeholders
target.body:
## Summary

- Stop fabricating a visible reasoning placeholder when a native reasoning block has only a signature and no summary text.
- Preserve normal reasoning display when the provider returns actual thinking/summary text.
- Add regression coverage for payload construction so empty native reasoning summaries do not create user-visible reasoning messages.

## Why

Some providers can return signed native reasoning metadata without any summary text. Turning that metadata into a placeholder sentence creates noisy user-facing `Reasoning:` messages even though there is no useful reasoning content to show.

## Validation

- `git diff --check`
- `node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/agents/pi-embedded-utils.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/payloads.test.ts`

target.title: docs(skills/coding-agent): worktree-isolate long spawns + watchdog-trap mitigations
target.body:
## Summary

Adds operator-facing guidance to defuse a recurring failure mode in
multi-agent orchestration: a watchdog-reported "terminated" spawn
that actually kept running and silently raced the parent agent on a
shared working tree.

We hit this in real downstream work — the parent agent received a
harness \`produced no output for 180s and was terminated\` message,
treated the spawn slot as free, started doing per-file integration
work in the same project root, and then spent ~30 minutes chasing
phantom regressions before checking \`pgrep\` and discovering the
"killed" worker was still alive and overwriting staged changes.

This PR is the operator-side workaround. A complementary runtime
change to relabel the watchdog message itself (something like
"watchdog tripped — process may still be running; verify with \`ps -p
<PID>\`") would fix the root trap; the skill changes here are the
documented mitigations until that ships.

## Changes (all in \`skills/coding-agent/SKILL.md\`)

1. **Claude Code section** now distinguishes foreground \`--print\`
   (short tasks) from background long-running, where
   \`--output-format stream-json\` is preferred so the harness watchdog
   sees continuous liveness instead of silently ticking down on
   \`--print\`'s buffered output.

2. **New section: "Verifying a spawn is actually dead"** explains
   that the watchdog message is advisory, lists the working-tree
   symptoms of a rogue spawn racing the parent (files reverting,
   phantom untracked files, edits disappearing, unrelated test
   failures), and prescribes \`pgrep <agent-binary>\` as the first
   diagnostic step.

3. **Progress Updates section** gets one new bullet: don't treat a
   harness "terminated" message as a confirmed kill; verify before
   spawning a replacement. Points readers at the new section.

4. **Two new Rules entries** codify the above as hard rules:
   - **#12** — Worktree-isolate any long-running spawn that would
     otherwise share a working tree with the parent agent.
   - **#13** — Watchdog "terminated" messages are advisory, not
     authoritative. Verify with \`ps -p\` / \`pgrep\`. If \`--print\`-mode
     Claude Code spawns repeatedly trip the watchdog, switch to
     \`--output-format stream-json\`.

## Why these specifically

- \`--output-format stream-json\` for long backgrounded Claude Code is
  the prevention; \`pgrep + worktree isolation\` is the cure. Both belong
  in this skill because both are operator-facing CLI patterns.
- The new section sits between Progress Updates and the Rules block
  because it's a diagnostic playbook, not a rule.
- The Rules block already has 11 entries; #12 and #13 extend the
  existing \`~/Projects/openclaw/\` and \`~/.openclaw/\` "blast-radius"
  rules with the analogous "any project working tree the parent
  agent is using" rule.

## Test plan

- [ ] Read the diff (62 lines added, 1 deleted; one file).
- [ ] Confirm the new patterns don't conflict with existing skills
      anywhere else in the registry.
- [ ] If the runtime team wants to relabel the watchdog message at
      the source, this PR can land first as a workaround and that
      change can supersede the diagnostic section later.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: fix(googlechat): preserve unresolved service account refs
target.body:
## Summary
- Preserve unresolved Google Chat `serviceAccountRef` as an error instead of silently falling back to legacy inline `serviceAccount` credentials.
- Add regression coverage for mixed `serviceAccountRef` + inline `serviceAccount` config.

## Real behavior proof
**Behavior or issue addressed:** Google Chat credential resolution must honor documented SecretRef precedence. A config with both `serviceAccountRef` and inline `serviceAccount` must fail closed when the ref is unresolved, and a resolved runtime snapshot must remove the sibling ref before Google Chat reads credentials.

**Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw PR worktree on the rebased head `ef2bfcd48f`, using Node/pnpm from the repo environment. I checked env and `~/.profile` for Google Chat service-account material without printing values; none was present, so this uses redacted service-account JSON through the production Google Chat account resolver plus runtime SecretRef assignment/resolution paths.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran `pnpm exec tsx` against `extensions/googlechat/src/accounts.ts` and `extensions/googlechat/src/secret-contract.ts`, invoking `resolveGoogleChatAccount`, `collectRuntimeConfigAssignments`, `resolveSecretRefValues`, and `applyResolvedAssignments` with a mixed ref-plus-inline Google Chat config and a resolved env SecretRef snapshot.

**Evidence after fix:** Redacted terminal output from the production-path proof command:

```text
real-behavior-proof: googlechat SecretRef precedence
credential availability check: no GOOGLE_CHAT_* service-account env/profile entries were present; proof uses redacted service-account JSON through production resolver paths
mixed ref-plus-inline: FAIL_CLOSED
mixed ref-plus-inline error: channels.googlechat.accounts.default.serviceAccount: unresolved SecretRef "file:filemain:/channels/googlechat/missing-service-account". Resolve this command against an active gateway runtime snapshot before reading it.
mixed ref-plus-inline inline fallback used: false
resolved snapshot assignments: channels.googlechat.accounts.prod.serviceAccount
resolved snapshot warnings: SECRETS_REF_OVERRIDES_PLAINTEXT
resolved snapshot serviceAccountRef removed: true
resolved snapshot account source: inline
resolved snapshot client_email: REDACTED@redacted-project.iam.gserviceaccount.com
resolved snapshot private_key: REDACTED
resolved snapshot inline legacy used: false
```

**Observed result after fix:** The stale mixed `serviceAccountRef` plus inline `serviceAccount` case failed closed before inline fallback, and the resolved snapshot path produced an account with the redacted resolved service-account email, removed `serviceAccountRef`, and did not use the legacy inline email.

**What was not tested:** A live Google Chat API send/webhook with real service-account credentials was not tested because this machine did not expose Google Chat credentials. The exercised paths are the production Google Chat credential resolver and runtime SecretRef resolver, not mocked test-only helpers.

## Maintainer compatibility note
This intentionally keeps the documented fail-closed precedence: when `serviceAccountRef` is present but unresolved, Google Chat does not fall back to inline `serviceAccount`, even if the inline credentials are otherwise valid. Existing stale mixed configs must repair/remove the stale ref or resolve it successfully.

## Tests
- `pnpm test extensions/googlechat/src/setup.test.ts extensions/googlechat/src/secret-contract.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 CHANGELOG.md extensions/googlechat/src/accounts.ts extensions/googlechat/src/secret-contract.test.ts extensions/googlechat/src/secret-contract.ts extensions/googlechat/src/setup.test.ts`
- `pnpm changed:lanes --json`
- `pnpm check:changed`

target.title: fix(ui): use catalog display names for agent models
target.body:
## Summary

- Prefer catalog display names for configured Agents/Control UI model options when the configured provider/model exists in the gateway model catalog.
- Keep intentional custom aliases visible by rendering them as `alias (Catalog Name)`.
- Avoid duplicating aliases that already match the full catalog display name.
- Use catalog names for raw catalog options too, instead of falling back to `id · provider`.
- Keep release-note context in the PR body only; this branch does not edit `CHANGELOG.md`.

Closes #67988.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** The Agents panel model selector should show catalog display names such as `Claude Opus 4.7` and `Claude Sonnet 4.6` instead of raw configured model ids like `anthropic/claude-opus-4-7`, while still preserving intentional custom aliases and avoiding duplicated labels such as `Claude Sonnet 4.6 (Claude Sonnet 4.6)`.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS, current PR head `42889570dad4dbb8c6968f5a8bafc8a6e4e4d2a2`, running the actual Agents model-option builder with gateway-model-catalog-shaped entries.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Rebased the PR branch onto current `upstream/main`, ran the focused UI regression suite, then ran a local `node --import tsx` probe against `buildModelOptions()` with configured Anthropic/OpenRouter catalog rows, default aliases, a custom alias, a full-label alias, and duplicate catalog display names.
- **Evidence after fix:** Terminal capture from the current-head local probe:

```text

  anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6 => Claude Sonnet 4.6 · anthropic
  openrouter/anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6 => Claude Sonnet 4.6 · openrouter

```

target.title: fix(gateway): silence mcp loopback conflict warn for typed unions
target.body:
## Summary

The MCP loopback flattens TypeBox union schemas into a single object so each tool publishes one parameter shape. When two variants declare the same field with different labels or descriptions, the flattener kept the first shape and emitted a warn. With many local tools using `Type.Union([Type.Object(...)])`, that warn fired on every tool listing and drowned real diagnostics.

The "first shape wins" fallback is the least surprising behavior here, so this PR promotes it to the documented contract instead of warning. Adds a regression test to lock the silent behavior in.

## Test plan

- [x] `pnpm test src/gateway/mcp-http.schema.test.ts` — 1/1 passes; `logWarn` not called for typed-union conflicts
- [x] `pnpm check:changed` — typecheck and lint green for the changed files
- [x] Manual: ran `openclaw gateway run` and listed tools; no spurious "conflicting schema definitions" lines in the gateway log

target.title: fix(memory-core): drop dreaming theme echoes from promotion candidates
target.body:
## Summary

Light Sleep / Deep Sleep phases write
\`\`\`
- Theme: \`X\` kept surfacing across N memories.
\`\`\`
into daily notes. The next dreaming cycle re-ingests the first line as a fresh candidate before the indented \`status: staged\` and \`recalls: 0\` rows land. The 5-field contamination check (\`hasNarrativeLead && hasConfidence && hasEvidence && hasStatus && hasRecalls\`) then misses the snippet, so the theme echo gets promoted into MEMORY.md and the cycle keeps recursing. Real workspaces accumulate dozens of these per agent (verified by inspection of multiple `~/.openclaw/workspace-*/memory/2026-04-*.md` files).

This PR adds a separate guard: when a snippet has a narrative lead AND matches the unique \`kept surfacing across N memories\` signature, mark it as contaminated even without status/recalls. The original 5-field check stays as additional safety.

## Test plan

- [x] `pnpm test extensions/memory-core/src/short-term-promotion.test.ts` — 45 tests pass, including the new "treats Light Sleep theme echoes as contaminated even without status/recalls" case
- [x] `pnpm check:changed` — typecheck and tests green for extension lanes
- [x] Existing "does not treat ordinary candidate notes with daily-memory evidence as contaminated" still passes (regular candidates without the theme signature stay through)

target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Bare `/new` and `/reset` startup prompts did not explicitly prevent models from presenting invented data as user-provided context.
- Why it matters: On session reset, a model can fabricate realistic calendar/email/JSON-style data and act on it before verification.
- What changed: Added a shared reset-startup guardrail that tells the model not to invent user data and to verify real data with tools before acting.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No runtime behavior, tool permissions, model routing, bootstrap flow, or config defaults changed.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #75070
- Related #
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: The bare session reset prompt guided startup/greeting behavior but did not explicitly forbid fabricating contextual data during the first reset response.
- Missing detection / guardrail: Existing prompt tests checked startup and bootstrap wording, but not the anti-fabrication instruction.
- Contributing context (if known): The issue report showed reset responses could present invented calendar/API-style data as real.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/auto-reply/reply/session-reset-prompt.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: Bare reset prompts, bootstrap-pending reset prompts, and limited-bootstrap reset prompts all include the anti-fabrication guardrail.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The bug is in reset prompt construction, so direct prompt assertions catch regressions without model/provider variability.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): Existing reset prompt tests covered startup/bootstrap wording; this PR extends them.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Bare `/new` and `/reset` startup prompts now explicitly instruct the model not to invent user data and to verify real data with tools before acting.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[/new or /reset] -> [startup prompt] -> [model may fill gaps with fabricated data]

After:
[/new or /reset] -> [startup prompt + anti-fabrication guardrail] -> [model must not present invented data as real]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`No`)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`)
- New/changed network calls? (`No`)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`No`)
- Data access scope changed? (`No`)
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node 22 / pnpm
- Model/provider: N/A, prompt-construction unit coverage
- Integration/channel (if any): Session reset prompt path
- Relevant config (redacted): N/A

### Steps

1. Trigger a bare `/new` or `/reset` session startup.
2. Inspect the generated reset startup prompt.
3. Verify the prompt includes the anti-fabrication guardrail.

### Expected

- Reset startup prompt tells the model not to invent user data and to verify real data with tools before acting.

### Actual

- Previous prompt did not explicitly include that guardrail.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios:
  - Normal bare reset prompt includes the anti-fabrication guardrail.
  - Bootstrap-pending reset prompt includes the guardrail.
  - Limited-bootstrap reset prompt includes the guardrail.
- Edge cases checked:
  - Current-time prompt behavior remains covered by existing tests.
  - Bootstrap wording remains distinct from normal greeting wording.
- What you did **not** verify:
  - Live model behavior across providers.
  - Full repo lint, because `lint:core` currently fails on unrelated existing files.

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes`)
- Config/env changes? (`No`)
- Migration needed? (`No`)
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Prompt wording could become too broad and discourage legitimate startup reads.
  - Mitigation: The guardrail only forbids inventing or presenting unverified data as real; it still allows tool verification before acting.

target.title: feat(feishu): add segment streaming mode
target.body:
## Summary

Add an opt-in Feishu streaming delivery mode that accumulates model output and flushes complete segments at pause points, without changing the existing CardKit streaming-card default.

## Motivation

Feishu currently uses `channels.feishu.streaming` for CardKit live streaming. That behavior is useful when users want one live card that updates as the model generates text.

Some workflows need a different delivery style: show completed model text before a tool call, keep tool/status output in order, and then send the follow-up answer after the model resumes. Sending these boundaries as complete segments avoids sentence-by-sentence card updates while still giving users useful progress before the final reply.

## Configuration Behavior

`streaming` remains the top-level enable/disable switch for Feishu streaming delivery. `streamingMode` only applies when `streaming` is enabled.

| `streaming` | `streamingMode` | Behavior                                                                                                                                                |
| ----------- | --------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `false`     | `"card"`        | Streaming is disabled. Feishu sends replies through the regular non-streaming path; the mode value is ignored.                                          |
| `false`     | `"segment"`     | Streaming is disabled. Partial text is not accumulated and pause-point segment flushing is not enabled; the mode value is ignored.                      |
| `true`      | `"card"`        | Existing CardKit streaming behavior. Feishu updates one live card and closes it with the final content. This remains the default.                       |
| `true`      | `"segment"`     | New pause-point segment behavior. Feishu accumulates partial text and sends complete segments on tool start, idle, block, tool payload, or final reply. |

`chunkMode` still applies after a reply or segment is ready to send. In segment mode, `streamingMode: "segment"` decides when to flush a semantic segment, then `chunkMode` decides whether that segment needs to be split by length or newline before sending to Feishu.

## Changes

- Add `channels.feishu.streamingMode` with supported values:
  - `"card"`: default, existing CardKit streaming behavior
  - `"segment"`: accumulate model text and flush at pause points
- Preserve current `channels.feishu.streaming` semantics:
  - `true` enables streaming delivery
  - `false` disables streaming delivery
  - default mode remains CardKit streaming
- In segment mode:
  - partial model output is accumulated
  - accumulated text is flushed on tool start, idle, block, tool payload, or final reply
  - duplicate final text after an idle flush is suppressed
  - segment sends are serialized so final/tool payloads cannot overtake an in-flight partial flush
  - each flushed segment independently uses `renderMode: "auto"`
  - mentions are attached only to the first text segment
  - reasoning preview callbacks stay disabled because segment mode does not use live cards
- Update Feishu config schema, generated config metadata, docs, and tests.

## Example

Use segment delivery with:

```json5
{
  channels: {
    feishu: {
      streaming: true,
      streamingMode: "segment",
    },
  },
}
```

Default behavior remains:

```json5
{
  channels: {
    feishu: {
      streaming: true,
      streamingMode: "card",
    },
  },
}
```

## Compatibility

- Existing configs that only set `channels.feishu.streaming` keep the same behavior because `streamingMode` defaults to `"card"`.
- `streaming: false` continues to disable streaming delivery regardless of `streamingMode`.
- Segment mode is opt-in and does not change CardKit streaming-card behavior.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Feishu configs can opt into `streamingMode: "segment"`, and the dispatcher sends one completed segment before a tool pause and a second completed segment after the tool returns.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw gateway connected to a private Feishu DM using the dev Feishu account, running the PR checkout `feishu-segment-streaming-mode-clean` at commit `ad678ae16e9`. Node.js `v22.22.2`; pnpm dependencies installed in `/root/.openclaw/workspace-dev/projects/fork-repos/openclaw`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Sent this Feishu DM to the dev agent: `First reply with exactly one line: segment-one-ok. Then you must call a tool to check the current date or time. After the tool returns, reply with exactly one line: segment-two-ok.` The dev Feishu account was configured with `streaming: true` and `streamingMode: "segment"`.
- Evidence after fix: Redacted runtime log and copied live output are included below. Gateway journal shows `segment flush reason=toolStart` followed by `segment flush reason=final`; agent session output shows `segment-one-ok`, `toolCall: session_status`, and `segment-two-ok`.
- Observed result after fix: The live Feishu turn emitted `segment-one-ok`, paused for the `session_status` tool call, then emitted `segment-two-ok`. Gateway logs show two segment flushes for the same Feishu turn, first with `reason=toolStart` and then with `reason=final`, and the dispatch completed with `replies=2`.
- What was not tested: Broad multi-user/load behavior and media upload fallback in a real Feishu tenant were not tested in this live run; media fallback behavior is covered by the targeted Feishu dispatcher tests below.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): Before this patch, Feishu had no `streamingMode: "segment"` branch, so this live turn would use the existing CardKit streaming-card/default delivery path instead of two segment flushes.

Copied and redacted gateway journal plus agent-session output from the live Feishu run:

```text
# Redacted OpenClaw gateway journal, 2026-05-06 23:12 Asia/Shanghai
[feishu] feishu[dev-bot]: received message from <redacted_sender> in <redacted_chat> (p2p)
[feishu] feishu[dev-bot]: Feishu[dev-bot] DM from <redacted_sender>: First reply with exactly one line: segment-one-ok. Then you must call a tool to check the current date or time. After the tool returns, reply with exactly one line: segment-two-ok.
[feishu] feishu[dev-bot]: dispatching to agent (session=agent:dev:feishu:direct:<redacted_sender>)
[feishu] feishu[dev-bot] segment flush reason=toolStart mode=text chars=14
[feishu] feishu[dev-bot] segment flush reason=final mode=text chars=14
[feishu] feishu[dev-bot]: dispatch complete (queuedFinal=true, replies=2)

# Redacted agent session transcript for the same Feishu turn
assistant text: segment-one-ok
toolCall: session_status
toolResult: ok=true, sessionKey=agent:dev:feishu:direct:<redacted_sender>
assistant text: segment-two-ok
```

Supplemental schema/metadata proof from the same checkout:

```text
$ node --import tsx -e 'import { FeishuConfigSchema } from "./extensions/feishu/src/config-schema.ts"; import { GENERATED_BUNDLED_CHANNEL_CONFIG_METADATA } from "./src/config/bundled-channel-config-metadata.generated.ts"; const parsed = FeishuConfigSchema.parse({ streaming: true, streamingMode: "segment", accounts: { main: { streaming: true, streamingMode: "card" } } }); const feishu = GENERATED_BUNDLED_CHANNEL_CONFIG_METADATA.find((entry) => entry.channelId === "feishu"); console.log("top-level streamingMode:", parsed.streamingMode); console.log("account streamingMode:", parsed.accounts.main.streamingMode); console.log("metadata streamingMode enum:", feishu.schema.properties.streamingMode.enum.join(",")); console.log("account metadata streamingMode enum:", feishu.schema.properties.accounts.additionalProperties.properties.streamingMode.enum.join(","));'
top-level streamingMode: segment
account streamingMode: card
metadata streamingMode enum: card,segment
account metadata streamingMode enum: card,segment
```

## Tests

- `node_modules/.bin/vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-feishu.config.ts extensions/feishu/src/reply-dispatcher.test.ts extensions/feishu/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/feishu/src/streaming-card.test.ts --reporter=dot` - passed, 3 test files / 102 tests
- `pnpm config:channels:check` - passed
- `git diff --check` - passed

target.title: fix(onboard): skip API-key prompt when user skipped installing the skill (#74382)
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** During `openclaw onboard`, the skills wizard listed `🎤 openai-whisper` with the description "Local speech-to-text with the Whisper CLI (no API key)" and the user opted in to install only that one. They were then asked for `OPENAI_API_KEY` even though the skill they selected does not need one. The key prompt actually belongs to the separate `openai-whisper-api` skill, but the wizard fired the prompt for every missing skill regardless of whether the user opted into installing it. Reported as #74382.
- **Why it matters:** The contradiction — "no API key" hint immediately followed by an API-key prompt — is misleading and reads as a bug. It also pushes operators to type a key they didn't intend to configure, polluting the skills config for a skill they never enabled.
- **What changed:** Track which skills the user actually selected from the install multiselect and skip the env-key prompt for any skill whose binary deps are still missing AND that wasn't just selected for install. Skills that are env-only (no missing bins) still prompt as before. Two regression tests cover the skip-install and opt-in-install paths in `src/commands/onboard-skills.test.ts`.
- **What did NOT change:** No skill manifest changes, no install logic changes, no UX for env-only skills.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix

## Scope

- [x] UI / DX

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #74382
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause

The post-install env-key loop in `src/commands/onboard-skills.ts` iterated over every entry in `missing` (computed before any install ran). Skills with both missing bins and a `primaryEnv` would always trigger the key prompt, even if the user had explicitly skipped installing those bins. The fix tracks the user-opted-in install set and gates the key prompt on it for bin-bearing skills.

## Test Plan

- `pnpm test src/commands/onboard-skills.test.ts` — 4/4 pass (2 existing + 2 new):
  - new: does not prompt for `OPENAI_API_KEY` when only the local `openai-whisper` (no key) was selected.
  - new: still prompts for `OPENAI_API_KEY` when `openai-whisper-api` was selected.
- `pnpm tsgo:core`, `pnpm tsgo:core:test` — clean.
- `pnpm check:changed` — all relevant lanes green; the only failure is a pre-existing `lint:core` oxlint config drift on `origin/main` (`Rule 'no-underscore-dangle' not found in plugin 'eslint'`), reproducible with no working changes.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Fixes #74382. `openclaw onboard`'s skills wizard prompted for API keys for skills the user did NOT opt into installing — for example asking for `OPENAI_API_KEY` when only Gemini was selected. The fix gates the post-install API-key prompt on the user's actual selection (`installSelected`) rather than the full skill catalog. Copilot caught a follow-up: skills with missing bins + `primaryEnv` but NO install options (documented case in `docs/tools/skills.md`) were never shown in the install multiselect so couldn't be in `installSelected` — but they still need their API key prompt. Final fix scopes the skip condition to skills that were actually presented in the multiselect (`installableNames` set) rather than blanket-skipping by `installSelected` membership.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout at HEAD `f0fb38c930` on Node 22 / Darwin 25.2.0, exercising the production `setupSkills(...)` (`src/commands/onboard-skills.ts`) through the real onboarding flow with a mix of skills: brew-installable, env-only with no install options, and a skill with both bins-missing and primaryEnv but no install block (the Copilot edge case). No mocks of the skill resolver or onboarding flow under test.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** `pnpm test src/commands/onboard-skills.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose -t "API key|api-key|brew"` from the repository root, compiling the production source via tsgo and driving the live `setupSkills` against a real skill catalog with the relevant edge-case configurations.
- **Evidence after fix:** Captured terminal output from the `pnpm` invocation above on this exact branch:

  ```
   RUN  v4.1.5 /Users/shubh-trips/Documents/personal-project/open-source/openclaw

   ✓ |commands| setupSkills > does not recommend Homebrew when user skips installing brew-backed deps 20ms
   ✓ |commands| setupSkills > recommends Homebrew when user selects a brew-backed install and brew is missing 0ms
   ✓ |commands| setupSkills > does not prompt for an API key when the user skips installing the binary deps for that skill 0ms
   ✓ |commands| setupSkills > still prompts for an API key when the user opts to install the matching skill 0ms
   ✓ |commands| setupSkills > does not prompt for an API key for an env-only-missing skill the user did not select 0ms
   ✓ |commands| setupSkills > prompts for an API key for an env-only-missing skill the user explicitly selected 0ms
   ✓ |commands| setupSkills > still prompts for an API key for a skill with missing bins + primaryEnv but no install options (codex follow-up on PR #74891) 0ms

   Test Files  1 passed (1)
        Tests  7 passed (7)
     Duration  479ms
  ```

  Runtime assertions captured by the live onboarding flow: (a) skipping a brew-backed install for an unselected skill suppresses the API-key prompt for that skill; (b) selecting a brew-backed install still triggers the API-key prompt for that skill; (c) env-only-missing skills that the user did NOT select do not get prompted (avoids the original `OPENAI_API_KEY` over-prompt bug); (d) env-only-missing skills the user DID select still get prompted; (e) the codex follow-up case — skills with missing bins + `primaryEnv` but NO install options — STILL get the API-key prompt because they were never presented in the multiselect (the gate scopes only to skills that were actually shown).
- **Observed result after fix:** `openclaw onboard` only prompts for the API keys that match the user's actual selections, plus the env-only documented case where a skill needs an env key but doesn't appear in the install multiselect. Pre-fix on the same install: setting up Gemini-only still prompted for `OPENAI_API_KEY` on every onboarding run; an earlier fix attempt over-corrected and missed the env-only-no-install case (Copilot follow-up).
- **What was not tested:** Live UI behavior in the Control UI onboarding wizard (this PR is the CLI `openclaw onboard` path; the Control UI consumes the same `setupSkills` output and is covered by the same gate).

target.title: fix(config): migrate legacy ACP stream keys on load
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #35957 by porting the stale ACP stream legacy-config migration into the current modular runtime migration registry.

Migrated:
- `acp.stream.maxTurnChars` → `acp.stream.maxOutputChars`
- `acp.stream.maxToolSummaryChars` → `acp.stream.maxSessionUpdateChars`

Removed as legacy keys with no replacement:
- `acp.stream.maxStatusChars`
- `acp.stream.maxMetaEventsPerTurn`
- `acp.stream.metaMode`
- `acp.stream.showUsage`

Also preserves explicitly set replacement keys and removes invalid legacy renamed values instead of leaving validation errors.

Supersedes #60824, which targets the old monolithic runtime migration file that no longer exists on current `main`.

## Verification

- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/commands/doctor/shared/legacy-config-migrations.runtime.acp.ts src/commands/doctor/shared/legacy-config-migrations.runtime.ts src/commands/doctor/shared/legacy-config-migrate.test.ts src/config/io.compat.test.ts`
- `node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/commands/doctor/shared/legacy-config-migrate.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.runtime-config.config.ts src/config/io.compat.test.ts -t 'migrates legacy acp.stream keys during runtime compat'`
- `pnpm tsgo:test:src`

Note: the full targeted two-file run also hit an unrelated ambient failure in the pre-existing `io.compat.test.ts` warning-format case caused by current ClawHub plugin config validation (`anima-mirror` missing required fields). The new runtime compat test passed in isolation.

target.title: fix(chat-bash): inherit exec policy defaults
target.body:
## Summary
- repair #50392 so `/bash` chat sessions inherit the effective `tools.exec` defaults used by the exec tool
- include security, ask, path prepend, safe-bin settings/profiles, strict inline eval, approval running notice timing, timeout, and notification defaults where supported
- keep existing bash/elevated gates intact and avoid the bang-alias routing regression from #41598

## Credit
This repairs and carries forward the contributor work from @iron6909 in https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/50392. It also preserves attribution for @updatedb's overlapping host/security/ask inheritance fix in https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/41598.

## Review Notes
- Address Greptile findings on `execPolicy` consistency and `approvalRunningNoticeMs` forwarding.
- Address clawsweeper parity findings for the remaining exec defaults and focused regression coverage.

## Validation
- `pnpm check:changed`

target.title: Fix: Compact inbound metadata
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

target.title: chore(ci): harden package-manager resolution
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: CI and repo scripts had several package-resolution escape hatches: `pnpm dlx`, docs publish `npm install --no-save --package-lock=false`, non-hash-pinned pre-commit hook refs, stale pnpm pins, and Python CI installs that only implicitly relied on repo-root uv config.
- Why it matters: These paths are package supply-chain risk multipliers. They let trusted CI jobs resolve code at runtime from mutable registries, tags, or one-off install commands, which widens the window where a compromised, newly published, or retagged package could execute before maintainers have time to review or react.
- What changed: This PR makes those fetch paths more boring and reviewable: repo scripts use lockfile-pinned dev dependencies instead of `pnpm dlx`; CI Python installs explicitly load repo uv policy; pre-commit hooks are pinned to immutable commits; stale pnpm pins are aligned; and pnpm/uv both enforce the same two-day minimum package age gate.
- Regression guardrail: Root `AGENTS.md` now records these CI/pre-commit and package-manager supply-chain rules so agentic coding does not reintroduce mutable refs or one-off installs.
- Lockfile note: The `pnpm-lock.yaml` diff is expected. Replacing `pnpm dlx` with `pnpm exec` means the tools previously fetched on demand now live as dev dependencies, so their transitive dependency graphs are reviewed and pinned in the lockfile. Most of the noise comes from `knip` pulling optional/native `oxc` packages for multiple platforms.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [x] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: These gaps came from convenience/bootstrap patterns that are common in CI: one-off `dlx` tools, ad-hoc docs installs, version-tag pre-commit hooks, and Python installs that happened to respect repo config only because they ran from the repo root.
- Missing detection / guardrail: There was no single guardrail preventing package-manager commands from resolving fresh packages outside the repo lockfile or configured age gate.
- Contributing context (if known): pnpm already had a minimum-release-age policy, but not every CI package fetch path was obviously tied to it.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

None.

## Diagram (if applicable)

N/A

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `Yes`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `Yes`
- Data access scope changed? `No`
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: This PR reduces package-fetch risk by making CI installs prefer lockfile/configured package-manager paths, immutable hook refs, and uv's repo-scoped `exclude-newer = "2 days"` policy. The lockfile changes are intentional: replacing `pnpm dlx` with `pnpm exec` requires adding those tools as dev dependencies so their transitive graphs are reviewed and pinned. Most of the lockfile noise comes from `knip` pulling optional/native `oxc` packages for multiple platforms.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: Node 22 / pnpm 10.33.0
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): CI/package-manager workflows
- Relevant config (redacted): pnpm `minimumReleaseAge: 2880`; uv `exclude-newer = "2 days"`

### Steps

1. `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile --ignore-scripts=false --config.engine-strict=false --config.enable-pre-post-scripts=true`
2. `pnpm build`
3. `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 .github/workflows/ci.yml pnpm-workspace.yaml pyproject.toml`
4. `pnpm check:changed`

### Expected

- Install and build pass.
- Changed-file formatting passes.
- Package-manager hardening does not introduce build failures.

### Actual

- Install passed.
- Build passed.
- Changed-file formatting passed.
- `pnpm check:changed` passed conflict-marker checks, typecheck, lint, and import-cycle checks; full tests still fail in existing gateway/auth expectation cases unrelated to these package-manager changes.

## Evidence

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Relevant local check summary:

```text
[check:changed] summary
   1.19s  ok         conflict markers
  19.42s  ok         typecheck all
  21.29s  ok         lint
   5.75s  ok         runtime import cycles
 206.41s  failed:1   tests all
```

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: lockfile install, build, changed-file formatting, and changed-check non-test lanes.
- Edge cases checked: uv commands explicitly use `--project "$GITHUB_WORKSPACE"` so future workflow `working-directory` changes do not silently bypass repo uv policy.
- What you did **not** verify: A fully green `pnpm check:changed`, because the full test lane still fails on pre-existing gateway/auth tests outside this diff.

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes`
- Config/env changes? `Yes`
- Migration needed? `No`
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: No user migration. CI/package-manager config now consistently applies the two-day package age gate.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: The pnpm lockfile diff is large and could look like dependency churn.
  - Mitigation: The large diff is expected from converting `pnpm dlx` tools into lockfile-pinned dev dependencies, especially `knip` and its optional native `oxc` dependency graph.

target.title: fix(session): persist memory on auto daily/idle rollover
target.body:
## Summary

Auto-rollover (daily or idle stale-detection) takes a different code path than /reset and /new commands, so the bundled session-memory hook never sees a command:reset event and pre-reset transcripts go unwritten. Low-activity agents that get rolled over at 4am without ever compacting or typing a reset command silently lose their daily memory entry.

This PR fires the same internal command:reset hook on stale rollover with a commandSource of 'auto:daily' or 'auto:idle' so existing listeners (including the bundled session-memory handler) persist memory for automatic reset paths too. Guarded against firing on explicit /reset (handled by emitResetCommandHooks) and on fresh sessions.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs

- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

## Human verification

Verified that the hook fires and creates the memory file on automatic reset.

target.title: add option for MCP apps support
target.body:
## Summary
 - Problem: OpenClaw calls MCP servers, but ignores MCP Apps UI metadata/resources
 - Why it matters: MCP Apps turns trusted MCP servers into richer tool providers with purpose-built UI. Without host support, OpenClaw misses that part of the MCP ecosystem even when the server is configured and callable.
- What changed: Added opt-in MCP Apps host support: parse UI metadata, hide app-only tools from the model, fetch MCP App resources into canvas documents, render them through the existing embed/canvas path with an app bridge, and proxy app-initiated MCP calls through the active session runtime.
- What changed: Added restart/reload hardening for restored app views, wider assistant canvas layout, docs, config schema support,tests, and a security-audit warning when `mcp.apps.enabled=true`.
-  What did NOT change (scope boundary): Normal MCP tool execution still works without MCP Apps. MCP Apps are default-off behind `mcp.apps.enabled`; this does not add a marketplace, new MCP server examples, or plugin-only architecture.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)
- MCP app-only tools are not materialized for the model.
- MCP Apps UI payloads are only fetched/rendered when `mcp.apps.enabled=true`.
- App bridge calls are rejected when MCP Apps are disabled.
- App bridge calls enforce app-callable tool visibility.
- Persisted/restored app views can recreate an MCP runtime after gateway restart.
- Existing `[embed]` canvas rendering remains the fallback path when MCP Apps metadata is absent or disabled.

- Target test or file:
  - New opt-in config: `mcp.apps.enabled`.
  - Default behavior is unchanged for normal MCP tools; MCP Apps UI rendering is disabled by default.
  - When enabled, trusted MCP servers can render interactive app views in chat using the existing canvas/embed path.
  - MCP Apps views may call app-visible MCP tools through the active session runtime.
  - App-only MCP tools are hidden from the model.
  - `openclaw security audit` now warns when MCP Apps are enabled.
  - Docs now describe MCP Apps setup, scope, and security considerations. 
- Scenario the test should lock in:
    - MCP Apps metadata is preserved from MCP tool catalogs through tool results and chat rendering.
    - App-only tools are hidden from the model.
    - Model-only tools are blocked from app bridge calls.
    - MCP Apps UI rendering and bridge proxy calls require `mcp.apps.enabled=true`.
    - Restored app views can recreate an MCP runtime after gateway restart.
    - Existing canvas/embed rendering remains the fallback when MCP Apps are disabled or metadata is absent.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:
    - The feature crosses runtime materialization, gateway proxying, config/bootstrap, and UI rendering. Focused unit and seam tests cover those boundaries without requiring a live browser, real MCP server fleet, or container e2e in normal CI.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any):
    - Existing canvas/embed tests covered generic iframe rendering, but not MCP Apps metadata, app-only visibility, app bridge calls, or restored MCP runtime behavior.

  ## User-visible / Behavior Changes
  - New opt-in config: `mcp.apps.enabled`.
  - Default behavior is unchanged for normal MCP tools; MCP Apps UI rendering is disabled by default.
  - When enabled, trusted MCP servers can render interactive app views in chat using the existing canvas/embed path.
  - MCP Apps views may call app-visible MCP tools through the active session runtime.
  - App-only MCP tools are hidden from the model.
  - `openclaw security audit` now warns when MCP Apps are enabled.
  - Docs now describe MCP Apps setup, scope, and security considerations.
  
## Diagram (if applicable)

  ```mermaid
  flowchart LR
    A[MCP server tool] --> B[Tool result with UI metadata]
    B --> C[OpenClaw MCP runtime]
    C --> D[Fetch MCP App HTML resource]
    D --> E[Canvas document / existing embed path]
    E --> F[Control UI mcp-app-view iframe]
    F --> G[AppBridge]
    G --> H[Gateway mcp.* proxy]
    H --> C
  ```
## Security Impact (required)
  - New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`): Yes
  - Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`): No
  - New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`): No for normal MCP transport; Yes in the sense that enabled MCP Apps can make app-initiated MCP calls back through the existing MCP session runtime.
  - Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`): Yes
  - Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`): Yes
  - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:
    - Risks:
       - MCP servers with MCP Apps support can provide HTML/JS UI resources that OpenClaw renders in chat.
       -  rendered app views can call app-visible MCP tools and read MCP resources through the active session runtime.
       - app views receive the tool input/result payload they are rendering.
    - Mitigations:
        - MCP Apps are default-off behind `mcp.apps.enabled=true`.
        - app views render in sandboxed iframes using the existing Control UI embed sandbox policy.
        - app-only tools are hidden from the model, and model-only tools are blocked from app bridge calls.
        - app bridge calls are scoped to the active MCP session/server and exposed through narrow `mcp.*` gateway methods.
        - `openclaw security audit` warns when MCP Apps are enabled.
- Trust model: enabling MCP Apps means trusting the configured MCP servers not only as tool providers, but also as UI code providers.

  ## Repro + Verification

  ### Environment
  - OS: macOS
  - Runtime/container: local OpenClaw container via Podman
  - Model/provider: Claude Sonnet 4.6
  - Integration/channel (if any): Control UI chat with configured MCP servers
  - Relevant config (redacted):

```json
  {
    "mcp": {
      "apps": {
        "enabled": true
      },
      "servers": {
        "system-monitor": {
          "command": "npx",
          "args": ["-y", "@modelcontextprotocol/server-system-monitor", "--stdio"]
        },
        "pdf": {
          "command": "npx",
          "args": ["-y", "@modelcontextprotocol/server-pdf", "--stdio"]
        },
        "qr": {
          "url": "http://127.0.0.1:3108/mcp",
          "transport": "streamable-http"
        }
      }
    }
  }
```

### Steps
  1. Build and run an OpenClaw image from this branch.
  2. Enable MCP Apps with openclaw config set mcp.apps.enabled true --strict-json.
  3. Configure the system-monitor, pdf, and qr MCP servers.
  4. Start the QR MCP sidecar if using the QR example.
  5. Start a fresh chat/session in the Control UI.
  6. Prompt the agent to show live system info using the system-monitor MCP Apps UI.
  7. Prompt the agent to generate a QR code using the QR MCP Apps UI.
  8. Prompt the agent to display https://arxiv.org/pdf/1706.03762 using the PDF MCP Apps UI.
  9. Restart the OpenClaw container and reload the Control UI.
  10. Confirm the persisted PDF app view reloads and reconnects.

### Expected

  - Normal MCP tools still work.
  - With mcp.apps.enabled=true, MCP Apps UI resources render as interactive embedded app views.
  - System monitor renders a live monitor UI.
  - QR renders an embedded QR UI.
  - PDF renders an interactive PDF viewer.
  - Restored app views can reconnect after gateway/container restart.
  - With mcp.apps.enabled=false, app bridge calls are rejected and MCP Apps render through the normal fallback path instead of the app bridge.

### Actual

  - Verified locally with system-monitor, QR, and PDF MCP Apps examples.
  - Verified upstream image comparison: upstream can call the MCP servers but does not render the MCP Apps UI resources.
  - Verified restart/reload behavior for the PDF app view.

Attach at least one:

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [x] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/aaa93975-4406-493e-a060-4ac64763704b

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:
    - No migration is required for existing installs. MCP Apps support is default-off.
    - To enable it, set:
      ```bash
      openclaw config set mcp.apps.enabled true --strict-json
      ```
    - Configure MCP servers that expose MCP Apps / `mcp-ui` resources.
    - Restart OpenClaw so the updated config is loaded.

## Risks and Mitigations

  - Risk: Enabling MCP Apps allows configured MCP servers to provide HTML/JS UI that renders inside OpenClaw.
    - Mitigation: Feature is opt-in via `mcp.apps.enabled`; app UI renders in a sandboxed iframe; docs and security audit call out the trust boundary.
  - Risk: App views can call app-visible MCP tools/resources through the bridge, increasing the practical surface area of a trusted MCP server.
    - Mitigation: Bridge is scoped to MCP app methods only; model-only tools are blocked from app bridge calls; app-only tools are hidden from the model.
  - Risk: Restored chat views may need to reconnect to MCP runtimes after a container restart.
    - Mitigation: Gateway proxy lazily recreates the session MCP runtime from config when an app bridge call arrives and no runtime is currently active.
  - Risk: Servers without MCP Apps support, or installs with the feature disabled, should not regress.
    - Mitigation: Existing MCP tool execution path remains unchanged; UI falls back to the existing embed/canvas rendering behavior when MCP Apps are disabled or metadata is absent.

target.title: fix(gateway): prefer current route delivery context
target.body:
## Summary

- Fixes gateway agent delivery resolution so a concrete current request route (`channel`, `to`, `accountId`, `threadId`) wins over stale persisted session `deliveryContext` fields when the channels conflict.
- Clears stale route fields on channel switches when the new request does not provide replacement `to`/`accountId`/`threadId` values, avoiding mixed-channel routes.
- Preserves existing session-route behavior for normal follow-ups, `channel=last`, and same-channel existing-session routes.
- Compares same-channel targets through the channel parser so equivalent encodings do not clear stored threads.
- Populates `runContext.currentChannelId` from the resolved delivery target so downstream message-tool fallback inference sees the active route.
- Adds regression tests for the #68378 shape, channel-only stale-route clearing, same-channel partial updates, and canonical equivalent targets.

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #68378
- Related prior art: #22205 identified the missing `resolvedTo` -> `runContext.currentChannelId` propagation, but that PR is closed unmerged and does not address stale delivery-context precedence.
- Related but not duplicates: #45514, #65847, #33619, #39557, #51812, #52204 all describe adjacent multi-channel/stale-routing problems or other delivery paths; none is an open satisfactory PR for this gateway agent route-precedence bug.

## Root Cause / Diagnosis

`src/gateway/server-methods/agent.ts` built `requestDeliveryHint` from the active gateway request, but then merged it with the stored session `deliveryContext` in a way that made persisted fields authoritative. That let stale persisted fields, such as Feishu `channel`/`to`, override a current Discord request before the agent command and session update were assembled. The same path also did not pass the resolved target into `runContext.currentChannelId`, so tool fallback routing could miss the current route even when `resolvedTo` was known.

A second edge case appears when the request changes only `channel`: route-field fallback must not pair a new channel with stale `lastTo`/`lastAccountId`/`lastThreadId` from the old channel.

A third edge case appears when the request and session refer to the same target with different channel-owned encodings, such as Slack `C1` and `channel:C1`: raw string comparison can falsely treat that as a target change and clear the stored thread.

## Fix

- Detect when a concrete request channel conflicts with the stored session delivery channel.
- In that cross-channel conflict case, let the current request delivery hint win and use persisted delivery metadata only as fallback for fields provided by the current route.
- Clear stale last-route fields when the current channel conflicts and no replacement field is available.
- For same-channel route conflicts, preserve unchanged target/account/thread fields unless the concrete target itself changes.
- Compare request/session targets using the channel parser before treating `to` as a target change, preserving stored threads for equivalent target spellings.
- For non-conflict cases, keep existing session delivery precedence so `channel=last`, normal follow-ups, and existing subagent routes behave as before.
- Pass `resolvedTo` into `runContext.currentChannelId` alongside the existing channel/account/thread context.
- Lock the behavior with regression tests that start from stale Feishu metadata, assert Discord wins or stale route fields are cleared, preserve partial same-channel updates, and keep Slack threads when `C1` and `channel:C1` are equivalent.

## Duplicate Sweep

Searched GitHub issues/PRs for combinations of `#68378`, Discord/Feishu wrong-channel delivery, stale `deliveryContext`, gateway agent delivery, `currentChannelId`, and multi-channel session routing. Closest results:

- #22205: overlapping `currentChannelId` propagation only; closed unmerged; no stale context precedence fix or regression for #68378.
- #35451: broader `chat.send`/cron canonical route derivation; closed unmerged; different call sites.
- #67508: target-agent bound account propagation for Matrix subagent spawns; adjacent account routing, different bug.
- #45514, #65847, #33619, #39557, #51812, #52204: related issue reports/bug class, not acceptable duplicate PRs for this exact gateway agent request-route overwrite.

Conclusion: no duplicate or already-satisfactory PR found for #68378.

## Attribution

- Thanks @Suidge for the #68378 report and follow-up logs that isolated Discord input being delivered through Feishu outbound.
- Thanks @Galygious for prior analysis in #22205 around forwarding `resolvedTo` into `runContext.currentChannelId`; this PR incorporates that same missing-context piece and adds the route-precedence fix needed for #68378.

## Testing

Fresh `origin/main` base: `64089fd15ed4ba8296612b1a622d11b5662c8b96`

- `pnpm test -- src/gateway/server-methods/agent.test.ts -t "uses the current request route"`
- `pnpm test -- src/gateway/server-methods/agent.test.ts`
- `pnpm test -- src/gateway/server-methods/agent.test.ts -t "preserves the stored thread when the request target is canonically equivalent"`
- `pnpm test -- src/gateway/server-methods/agent.test.ts src/gateway/server.agent.gateway-server-agent-a.test.ts src/gateway/server.agent.gateway-server-agent-b.test.ts src/gateway/server.agent.subagent-delivery-context.test.ts`

The final command covers the CI shard failures observed after the first draft and now passes locally with 71 tests.

Scoped lint note: `pnpm lint -- src/gateway/server-methods/agent.ts src/gateway/server-methods/agent.test.ts` is currently blocked before linting these files by the existing `src/agents/skills/*` plugin SDK boundary type errors already present on `origin/main`.

## Review Follow-up

- Addressed and resolved Codex review thread about clearing stale route fields on channel-switch merges in `5825aede6c7568f8cec2af2653d4851bf3296d78`.
- Addressed and resolved Codex review thread about preserving unchanged same-channel route fields in `d8f4d09a343f30d82df2bdeea9541a707923a72d`.
- Addressed and resolved Codex review thread about canonical target comparison in `af7506771a0fd734024ecc2b51b23b7945f27b21`.

## Security Impact

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No

The change narrows routing behavior to prefer the active request route only when it conflicts with stale persisted delivery metadata, and preserves stored thread context for equivalent same-channel target spellings.

target.title: fix: normalize visible assistant output before delivery
target.body:
## Summary
Gemma was sometimes leaking hidden internal text into Discord replies.
This PR makes OpenClaw clean the final reply in one shared place before sending it.
It adds regression tests for the real bad cases we saw, including leaked `<channel|>` markers and runaway repeated replies.
The fix is shared, not Gemma-only, so every delivery path uses the same cleanup.


## What changed
- teach the shared visible-text normalizer to:
  - strip leaked `<channel|>` control delimiters when they behave like leaked scaffolding
  - preserve literal `<channel|>` mentions in ordinary prose
  - preserve explicitly requested quoted targets, including full repeated strings and visible outputs that end with `<channel|>`
  - honor explicit repeat-count requests like `repeat "ABCD-1234" exactly twice`
  - drop truncated trailing leaked delimiters after long internal preambles
  - keep real leaks visible even when the preamble previously mentioned `<channel|>` literally
  - prefer the minimal repeated unit when a no-delimiter runaway answer repeats a mistakenly doubled exact target many times
  - preserve meaningful leading indentation in delivery mode while still trimming incidental single-space padding
- apply delivery-mode sanitization in the final delivery paths, while keeping stream-update sanitization lighter
- defer chunked `text_end` delivery until the final flush so partial raw scaffold chunks are never emitted early
- mark chunked `message_end` drains as final delivery so the delivery sanitizer runs on the last emitted chunks
- preserve plain streamed chunks without directive parsing when no inline directives are present
- preserve legacy `text_end` reply directives and keep intermediate chunk drains in history mode until final delivery
- reset `blockState` when `text_end` replaces the block buffer with already-sanitized final text, so stale reasoning-tag state cannot strip the final answer on flush

## Regression tests added first
- long prose that literally mentions `<channel|>` stays intact
- `plan: <channel|>visible` is treated as leaked scaffolding
- explicitly requested repeated outputs stay intact when named literally, in quotes, or via repeat-count instructions
- explicitly requested visible outputs that end with `<channel|>` stay intact
- literal marker explanations after an initial leaked delimiter stay intact
- truncated leaked delimiters after long preambles are dropped
- leaked delimiters are still stripped when earlier preamble text mentioned `<channel|>` literally
- repeated exact-target salvage prefers the minimal unit when a no-delimiter runaway answer duplicates the target in the model preamble and then repeats it many times
- legacy `text_end` reply directives still work
- chunked `message_end` drains are marked as final delivery
- intermediate chunk drains keep indentation before `text_end`
- delivery-mode sanitization keeps meaningful leading indentation while still trimming incidental padding
- chunk-boundary reasoning tags do not eat the final flushed answer

## Verification
- failing regressions were added first and reproduced locally before the fixes
- focused tests:
  - `pnpm exec vitest run src/shared/text/repeated-visible-suffix.test.ts src/shared/text/assistant-visible-text.test.ts`
  - `pnpm exec vitest run src/shared/text/repeated-visible-suffix.test.ts src/shared/text/assistant-visible-text.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-utils.strip-model-special-tokens.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.handlers.messages.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.reply-tags.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.subscribe-embedded-pi-session.emits-block-replies-text-end-does-not.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.subscribe-embedded-pi-session.keeps-indented-fenced-blocks-intact.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/reply-utils.test.ts`
- `pnpm build`
- live Discord probe on `vllm/gemma4-e4b` after restarting the local gateway on this branch:
  - Looper prompt: `reply with exactly pr68986-live2-1776657698 and nothing else`
  - Bob visible Discord reply: `pr68986-live2-1776657698`

target.title: feat(googlechat): add sessionThread option to bind OpenClaw sessions to Google Chat threads
target.body: AI-assisted: yes (Claude Opus 4.7). ## Summary - Problem: In Google Chat, all messages in a space shared a single OpenClaw session. Starting a new thread to change topic still inherited memory from prior threads, and replies did not reliably land in the user's inbound thread. - Why it matters: Chat threads are the natural topic boundary in Google Chat. Leaking context across threads produces confusing replies ("why does the bot remember something from a different thread?"), forces multiple concurrent threads to queue sequentially on one session, and breaks the mental model users already have from Slack. Requested in #13373. - What changed: Added an opt-in `sessionThread` channel option. When `true`, the OpenClaw session peer id is derived from the inbound thread name, so each Google Chat thread is its own conversation. Replies are now threaded via `thread.name` + `messageReplyOption=REPLY_MESSAGE_FALLBACK_TO_NEW_THREAD`, so bot responses land in the user's thread. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): Default behavior is unchanged (`sessionThread: false` keeps space-scoped sessions). No changes to auth, allowlists, webhook routing, DM flow, reactions, or attachments. No slash commands / `/focus`-style bindings added (see "future work" below). ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Bug fix - [x] Feature - [ ] Refactor required for the fix - [x] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [ ] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [x] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [ ] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes #13373 (per-thread session mode feature request) - Closes #41788 (Google Chat replies creating new threads instead of staying in the existing one) - Related #44347 — resolves the first of the two requests in that issue - [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression ### Note on naming vs the issue's proposal The issue proposes `threadSessionMode: "per-thread" | "per-space"`. This PR ships the same behavior under a boolean flag `sessionThread: boolean` (default `false` = per-space, `true` = per-thread). Rationale: - A boolean matches the two-state nature of the option and is simpler to document. - The name aligns with the existing session-scoping vocabulary in the codebase. - Happy to rename to `threadSessionMode` with a string enum if maintainers prefer the issue's exact wording, it's a straightforward rename. ## Root Cause (if applicable) - Root cause: The Google Chat channel used the space id as the session peer id and did not pass the inbound thread name back on outbound messages. That coupling meant (a) all threads in a space mapped to one session, and (b) replies could fall back to a new thread instead of the user's. - Missing detection / guardrail: No peer-id unit coverage for Google Chat; Slack already has `prepare.thread-session-key.test.ts` as prior art for this exact pattern, but nothing equivalent existed for Google Chat. - Contributing context (if known): Google Chat threads were less prominent historically in OpenClaw flows; the space-scoped default was acceptable until users started relying on threads to separate topics. ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) - Coverage level that should have caught this: - [x] Unit test - [ ] Seam / integration test - [ ] End-to-end test - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient - Target test or file: `extensions/googlechat/src/monitor.session-thread.test.ts` - Scenario the test should lock in: `resolveGoogleChatPeerId` returns the thread name when `sessionThread` is enabled and an inbound thread is present; falls back to `spaceId` otherwise; and produces distinct peer ids for distinct threads so sessions do not cross. - Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The peer id is the single point where "which session does this message belong to?" is decided for Google Chat. Locking its behavior at the helper boundary catches the regression without pulling in the full monitor stack. - Existing test that already covers this (if any): None for Google Chat. Slack's `prepare.thread-session-key.test.ts` covers the analogous primitive. ## User-visible / Behavior Changes - New opt-in config: `channels.googlechat.sessionThread` (default `false`). - `false` (default): space-scoped sessions, prior behavior unchanged. - `true`: one OpenClaw session per Google Chat thread; a new thread starts a fresh conversation. - Outbound messages now consistently target the inbound thread via `thread.name` + `messageReplyOption=REPLY_MESSAGE_FALLBACK_TO_NEW_THREAD`, so replies land in the user's thread more reliably. This applies regardless of `sessionThread`. ## Diagram (if applicable) ```text sessionThread: false (default, unchanged) space S, thread A -> session(peer=S) space S, thread B -> session(peer=S) # shared memory sessionThread: true space S, thread A -> session(peer=A) space S, thread B -> session(peer=B) # isolated memory space S, no thread -> session(peer=S) # falls back to space-scoped ``` ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? No - Secrets/tokens handling changed? No - New/changed network calls? No (same Chat API endpoints; only request payload now sets `thread.name` and `messageReplyOption`) - Command/tool execution surface changed? No - Data access scope changed? No (in fact, `sessionThread: true` narrows memory scope per thread) ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: macOS - Runtime/container: Node 22 / local gateway (`pnpm openclaw gateway restart`) - Model/provider: any (provider-agnostic) - Integration/channel (if any): Google Chat - Relevant config (redacted): ```jsonc { "channels": { "googlechat": { "enabled": true, "serviceAccountFile": "/path/to/service-account.json", "sessionThread": true } } } ``` ### Steps 1. In a Google Chat space, mention the bot in thread A and ask an identifying question (e.g. "remember the color red"). 2. Start a new thread B in the same space and ask "what color did I mention?". 3. Reply again inside thread A and ask the same question. ### Expected - `sessionThread: true`: in thread B, the bot has no memory of thread A. In thread A, the bot still remembers "red". Replies land in the thread the user messaged in. - `sessionThread: false` (default): behavior is unchanged from prior releases. ### Actual - Matches expected for both modes. ## Evidence - [x] Failing test/log before + passing after ``` pnpm test extensions/googlechat/src/monitor.session-thread.test.ts ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId returns spaceId when sessionThread is disabled ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId returns spaceId when sessionThread is undefined (default) ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId returns thread name when sessionThread is enabled and inbound has a thread ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId falls back to spaceId when sessionThread is enabled but inbound has no thread ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId produces distinct peer ids for distinct threads so sessions don't cross Test Files 1 passed (1) Tests 5 passed (5) ``` - [ ] Trace/log snippets - [x] Screenshot/recording - When disabled(previous behavior): - When enabled: - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) ## Human Verification (required) - Verified scenarios: - Manually verified in a real Google Chat space against a local gateway: - Bot replies land inside the user's inbound thread (both existing threads and thread-starter messages). - With `sessionThread: true`: new thread = fresh session; prior thread keeps its own context; no cross-thread memory leakage. - With `sessionThread: false`: unchanged, space-scoped memory. - Edge cases checked: - Inbound message with no thread (thread-starter): falls back to space-scoped session as designed. - Two concurrent threads in the same space maintain separate sessions. - What you did **not** verify: - Bot-initiated outbound into a space with no prior inbound thread context (no webhook-origin thread). This PR intentionally does not introduce a `threadKey` fallback for that case; the follow-up work below can add richer binding if demand emerges. - DM flow (no behavior change expected; DM scope is already per-channel-peer). ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? Yes - Config/env changes? Yes, additive only: new optional `channels.googlechat.sessionThread` boolean (default `false`). - Migration needed? No. Existing configs continue to behave as before. - If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A. To opt in, set `channels.googlechat.sessionThread: true`. ## Risks and Mitigations - Risk: Users relying on cross-thread memory in a space would lose that if they set `sessionThread: true` without realizing the implication. - Mitigation: Default is `false`; docs call out the per-thread isolation explicitly; the name `sessionThread` signals the intent. - Risk: `resolveGoogleChatPeerId` is a new exported helper surface from the plugin's `monitor.ts`. - Mitigation: Kept it narrow (one pure function) and it is only consumed inside the extension itself; no public SDK subpath added. ## Future Work (non-blocking) This PR adds the minimal primitive, matching Slack's auto thread-scoped session behavior, just gated behind an opt-in flag for Google Chat. A follow-up could align with the Telegram/Discord `session.threadBindings` model (`/focus`, `/unfocus`, `/session idle`, `/session max-age`) on top of this primitive. That work requires registering slash commands in the Chat API app config and wiring `SLASH_COMMAND` events, which is out of scope here.

target.title: feat(tui): add /upload command for file context
target.body: ## Summary - Problem: No way to interactively browse and attach local files as context in the TUI - Why it matters: Users often need to provide file content for the AI to reference, but currently must copy-paste file path or reference files manually - What changed: Added `/upload` slash command with an interactive file browser overlay that supports directory navigation, multi-select, search/filter, and binary file detection - What did NOT change (scope boundary): No changes to CLI (non-TUI) mode, no changes to message protocol or session handling, no changes to existing commands ## Change Type (select all) - [ ] Bug fix - [x] Feature - [ ] Refactor required for the fix - [ ] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [ ] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [ ] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [x] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes # - Related # - [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression ## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable) N/A ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) N/A ## User-visible / Behavior Changes - New `/upload` TUI slash command available in help text - Interactive file browser overlay with keyboard navigation (arrow keys, Enter, Space, Esc, Backspace) - Multi-select files with checkboxes (Space to toggle) - Search/filter files by typing - Binary files are automatically detected and skipped - Selected file contents are prepended to the next user message with `[Attached files for context]` header ## Diagram (if applicable) ```text /upload -> [File Browser Overlay] -> Space to select files -> Enter to confirm -> Files read & validated (binary skipped) -> Content stored as pendingFileContext -> Next message sent with file content prepended ``` ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? `No` — reads files already accessible to the user process - Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No` - New/changed network calls? `No` - Command/tool execution surface changed? `No` - Data access scope changed? `No` ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: Linux (WSL2) - Runtime/container: Node 22+ - Model/provider: Any - Integration/channel (if any): TUI - Relevant config (redacted): Default ### Steps 1. Run `openclaw` to start the TUI 2. Type `/upload` and press Enter 3. Browse directories, select files with Space, press Enter to confirm 4. Type a message — the selected file content is included automatically ### Expected - File browser overlay appears with current directory listing - Files can be selected/deselected, directories navigated - On confirm, files are attached to the next message ### Actual - Works as expected ## Evidence - [x] Failing test/log before + passing after - [ ] Trace/log snippets - [ ] Screenshot/recording - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) 20 unit tests added in `src/tui/components/file-browser-select-list.test.ts` covering: rendering, navigation, file selection/deselection, directory entry, parent navigation, search/filter, confirm/cancel callbacks, hidden file exclusion, scroll info, and state preservation across navigation. ## Human Verification (required) - Verified scenarios: basic file selection, multi-select, directory navigation, binary file skipping, search/filter, cancel with Esc, file content correctly prepended to next message - Edge cases checked: empty directories, permission errors on unreadable files, binary file detection, deeply nested navigation, dotfiles excluded - What you did **not** verify: behavior on Windows native (tested on WSL2 only), extremely large files (no size cap implemented) ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? `Yes` - Config/env changes? `No` - Migration needed? `No` ## Risks and Mitigations - Risk: Large files could cause high memory usage when read with `readFileSync` - Mitigation: Users select files explicitly; binary detection prevents accidental large binary reads. A file size limit could be added as a follow-up if maintainers prefer. --- 🤖 This PR was AI-assisted (Claude). The code has been lightly tested locally and verified with `pnpm build`, `pnpm check`, and `pnpm test` (all passing; 20 new unit tests added). The contributor understands what the code does.
