```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts src/gateway/startup-runtime-state.test.ts src/gateway/server-channels.test.ts

2 test files passed
40 tests passed
```

Typecheck note:
```bash
pnpm exec tsc --noEmit --pretty false -p tsconfig.json

Normal and 8GB heap attempts crashed due to Node/V8 heap/runtime failure.
No actionable TypeScript diagnostics were emitted.
```
## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

 - Verified scenarios:
    - runtime-state redaction behavior
    - redaction idempotency
    - channel startup success summary
    - immediate channel startup rejection summary
    - channel timeout summary
    - startup-doctor no longer reads source config / openclaw.json
explicit readiness object is present
 - Edge cases checked:
    - already-redacted values
    - punctuation/bracket endings
    - bearer/JWT-like/token/password/API key/credentialed URL style strings
    - one channel failing while later channel still starts
 - What you did not verify:
    - live third-party channel startup against real external services
    - full typecheck, because both normal and 8GB heap runs crashed with Node/V8 heap/runtime failure
## Review Conversations
  - I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
  - I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

### Compatibility / Migration
 - Backward compatible? (Yes)
 - Config/env changes? (No)
 - Migration needed? (No)
 - If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A
### Risks and Mitigations
 - Risk: timed-out channel startup tasks may continue in the background if they cannot be cancelled.
  - Mitigation: timeout state is explicitly reported via channel results/counters and documented.
 - Risk: consumers may confuse HTTP availability with full gateway readiness.
  - Mitigation: startup-doctor now exposes readiness.fullyReady; docs tell consumers not to rely on httpHealthy alone.
 - Risk: runtime-state errors could accidentally include sensitive data.
  - Mitigation: runtime-state strings are redacted before truncation and covered by focused tests.
 - Risk: full repo typecheck could not be completed in this environment due Node/V8 heap/runtime crash.
    - Mitigation: focused tests and diff checks passed; typecheck crash is documented as tooling/runtime blocked with no surfaced TypeScript diagnostics.


A small note: under **Scope**, I checked **API / contracts** because startup-doctor JSON/runtime-state shape is effectively a diagnostic contract, and **UI / DX** because this improves CLI/operator diagnostics.

target.title: fix(status): surface missing external channel plugins in summary
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`status` summary generation could still drop configured official external channels when their plugin owner was missing.

The previous patch only appended the missing-plugin repair hint inside `buildChannelSummary()`, but `getStatusSummary()` could still short-circuit before summary building when it only saw the runtime-resolved config shape. That left a gap where authored/source config still declared an official external channel, but summary mode emitted no repair hint.

This follow-up makes status summary use the same source-config view across the caller gate and summary builder, and updates announce-path detection to read authored external channel declarations from `activationSourceConfig` when the runtime config has already been normalized.

## Evidence

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/plugins/channel-plugin-ids.test.ts src/commands/status.summary.test.ts src/infra/channel-summary.test.ts`
  - `Test Files  3 passed (3)`
  - `Tests  182 passed (182)`

## Real behavior proof

- `Behavior addressed:`
  Status summary no longer drops configured official external channels solely because the runtime config view no longer carries the authored external channel declaration; the caller gate and builder now use the same source-config view.

- `Real environment tested:`
  Local OpenClaw source checkout on macOS, plus packaged-environment investigation for externalized channel ownership behavior.

- `Exact steps or command run after this patch:`
  - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/plugins/channel-plugin-ids.test.ts src/commands/status.summary.test.ts src/infra/channel-summary.test.ts`

- `Evidence after fix:`
  - Added caller-level coverage in `src/commands/status.summary.test.ts` for projected source-config channel summary behavior.
  - Added announce-path coverage in `src/plugins/channel-plugin-ids.test.ts` for activation-source-only external channel declarations.
  - `src/infra/channel-summary.test.ts` continues to assert the rendered repair hint:
    - `Missing official external plugins:`
    - `  - Feishu: Install the official external plugin with: openclaw plugins install @openclaw/feishu, or run: openclaw doctor --fix.`

- `Observed result after fix:`
  Targeted status-summary, channel-summary, and channel announce-path regressions all pass locally after the fix.

- `What was not tested:`
  A shipped-package real CLI proof for the exact missing-owner line was investigated, but source-checkout runtime and packaged-install runtime expose different channel-owner visibility, so this PR currently relies on focused regression coverage rather than a final redacted terminal capture of the exact summary line.

target.title: fix(scripts): warn when postinstall detects running gateway with stale module imports
target.body:
## Summary

After `npm install -g openclaw` replaces dist files (upgrade, rollback, or reinstall), the running gateway process holds ESM module caches pointing to the old hash-named dist files. Inbound messages fail silently with `ERR_MODULE_NOT_FOUND` — the process appears healthy (systemd reports active/running, channels show connected) but messages are silently dropped.

This fix adds a best-effort TCP probe to `127.0.0.1:18789` during postinstall. When a gateway is detected, it prints a visible ASCII warning box suggesting `openclaw gateway restart`.

Users can suppress the warning with `OPENCLAW_DISABLE_POSTINSTALL_RESTART_WARNING=true`.

Fixes #92241

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

**Behavior addressed**: Postinstall script now warns when a gateway is running with stale module imports.

**Real setup tested**:
- **Runtime**: WSL2 Ubuntu (Linux 6.6.87.2-microsoft-standard-WSL2), Node v22.22.2

**Exact steps or command run after fix**:

```bash
$ pnpm test -- test/scripts/postinstall-bundled-plugins.test.ts
```

**After-fix evidence**:

```
. postinstall$ node scripts/postinstall-bundled-plugins.mjs
. postinstall:   ╔══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗
. postinstall:   ║  WARNING: OpenClaw gateway restart recommended              ║
. postinstall:   ║  ...
. postinstall:   ║  Run:  openclaw gateway restart                              ║
. postinstall:   ╚══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝
. postinstall: Done
```

The warning appeared automatically — the postinstall detected the development gateway running on port 18789.

**Observed result after the fix**: On each `npm install -g` (or `pnpm install` triggering postinstall), if the gateway is running on the default port, users will see a clear ASCII warning suggesting restart. The check is best-effort with 1s timeout — it silently skips if the gateway isn't running or is on a non-default port.

**What was not tested**: Gateways on non-default ports, behind TLS proxies, or in containers. The TCP probe only checks 127.0.0.1:18789. A running gateway on a custom port would not trigger the warning.

## Tests and validation

39/39 tests passed (all existing + no regression):

```
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  39 passed (39)
   Start at  14:21:38
   Duration  1.91s
```

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes` / `No`)

**Yes** — a console warning is printed when a running gateway is detected during postinstall. This is the intended improvement.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes` / `No`)

**No** — existing env flags (`OPENCLAW_DISABLE_BUNDLED_PLUGIN_POSTINSTALL`) continue to work. A new opt-out env var (`OPENCLAW_DISABLE_POSTINSTALL_RESTART_WARNING`) is additive only.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes` / `No`)

**Minimal** — a TCP connect to 127.0.0.1:18789 with 1s timeout. No data is sent or received. The socket is destroyed immediately on successful connect. Loopback-only, no external network access.

What is the highest-risk area?
- Unexpected false positive: a non-gateway process listening on 18789 could trigger the warning. False negatives (missed gateway on non-default port) are safe — no warning is better than a wrong warning.

How is that risk mitigated?
- The warning text is advisory only ("restart recommended"), not an error. A false positive causes no harm beyond a visual suggestion. The 1s timeout prevents the postinstall from hanging.

## Current review state

What is the next action?
- Maintainer review

target.title: fix(cron): don't classify a yielded isolated-cron turn as a fatal error
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where an isolated-session scheduled (cron) agent run that ends by yielding — for example, after spawning subagents and pausing for them to complete — is reported as a fatal `error` and its delivery is suppressed, whenever a *non-fatal* tool error occurred earlier in the same turn (e.g. a shell `exec` that exits non-zero as benign control flow, like a `grep` with no match).

The run's actual work succeeds, but the schedule shows `error` / `consecutiveErrors` and no message is delivered.

## Why This Change Was Made

`resolveCronPayloadOutcome` only recognizes recovery from an earlier error via (a) a later successful payload or (b) a final assistant answer. A clean `sessions_yield` produces neither — a yielded turn emits no final assistant reply — so the earlier non-fatal error payload remains the de-facto terminal payload and drives `hasFatalStructuredErrorPayload = true`.

`sessions_yield` was never accounted for in the cron-outcome classifier; yield-awareness had previously been added only for media completions, not the textual/structured-payload path.

The fix threads the runner's existing `meta.yielded` flag into the classifier and treats a clean yield (no `runLevelError`, no fatal `failureSignal`) as a recovering, non-terminal outcome. A genuinely fatal `failureSignal`/`runLevelError` still flips the run fatal — yield-awareness does not mask real failures.

Non-goal: this does not re-deliver a digest assembled *after* spawned subagents finish (that path is a separate, deliberate short-circuit). Scope is strictly the misclassification of the yielded turn itself.

## User Impact

A scheduled isolated-agent run that legitimately yields is now reported as `ok` and its pre-yield output is delivered, instead of being marked failed with delivery suppressed. Operators stop seeing false `error` / `consecutiveErrors` on schedules that are actually working.

## Evidence

Two focused unit tests added to `src/cron/isolated-agent.helpers.test.ts`, written test-first (confirmed RED before the fix, GREEN after):

- `treats a clean sessions_yield as non-fatal despite an earlier benign tool error` — reproduces the bug: a benign `⚠️ 🛠️ … failed` payload before a clean yield no longer forces a fatal classification, and the successful pre-yield payload is preserved for delivery.
- `still marks a yielded turn fatal when a fatal failureSignal is present` — guard proving yield-awareness does not mask a genuinely fatal run.

```
 Test Files  108 passed (108)
      Tests  1144 passed (1144)
```
(full `vitest.cron` suite — no regressions; `oxlint` and `tsgo` core typecheck clean)

---
*Disclosure: prepared with AI assistance (Claude Code); authored and reviewed by the submitter.*

target.title: fix(agent-runtime): guard prompt cache tool names
target.body:
## Summary

- Harden prompt-cache observability against hostile tool-name descriptors.
- Treat unreadable and non-string tool names as absent while preserving the existing trim/filter behavior for healthy tools.
- Keep the change scoped to diagnostics metadata; runtime tool behavior is unchanged.
- Out of scope: changing prompt-cache affinity keys, cache-break thresholds, or tool projection rules.

## Linked context

Which issue does this close?

Closes #

Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related?

Related #

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

Ongoing tool/schema fuzzing hardening work.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior addressed: prompt-cache diagnostics can no longer crash when an active tool exposes an unreadable `name` descriptor.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw source worktree on Node via nodenv, branch `fuzz-tool-schema-next176-20260604`, commit `b7294fa9214c705340688020b9e30ddaa2740d20`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_FS_MODULE_CACHE_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-vitest-next176-rebased nodenv exec node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/prompt-cache-observability.test.ts --reporter=verbose`
- Evidence after fix: `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/prompt-cache-observability.test.ts` passed 1 file / 8 tests after the patch.
- Observed result after fix: unreadable and non-string prompt-cache tool names are skipped, while readable names are still trimmed and included.
- What was not tested: broad `pnpm check:changed` did not run because Blacksmith Testbox is not authenticated and AWS Crabbox returned coordinator 401 before lease creation.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: broad remote validation is blocked by maintainer-runner auth in this environment.
- Before evidence: the new focused repro failed pre-fix with `prompt cache tool name exploded`.

## Tests and validation

- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_FS_MODULE_CACHE_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-vitest-next176-red nodenv exec node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/prompt-cache-observability.test.ts --testNamePattern="skips unreadable" --reporter=verbose`

- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_FS_MODULE_CACHE_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-vitest-next176-rebased nodenv exec node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/prompt-cache-observability.test.ts --reporter=verbose`

If no test was added, why not?

Not applicable.

## Risk checklist

target.title: fix(doctor): isolate channel doctor hook failures
target.body:
## Summary

- Prevent channel plugin doctor hooks from crashing doctor preview, repair, mutable allowlist, stale cleanup, and config-sequence paths.
- Convert plugin-owned doctor hook failures into sanitized channel-scoped warnings where doctor has a warning surface.
- Keep core empty-allowlist warnings active when a plugin skip hook throws.
- Block plugin install recovery when stale channel cleanup reports warnings, because that flow has no warning panel and continuing would hide an untrusted cleanup failure.

## Linked context

Which issue does this close?

Closes #

Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related?

Related: ongoing tool-schema/doctor hardening fuzzing from the unsupported active tool schema report.

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

Maintainer fuzzing lane.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

Behavior addressed: a malformed channel plugin doctor callback can no longer abort doctor diagnostics before later healthy plugin warnings or active tool-schema warnings can be surfaced.

Real environment tested: local linked `gwt` worktree from current `origin/main`, Node/Vitest through `scripts/run-vitest.mjs`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_FS_MODULE_CACHE_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-vitest-next174-red nodenv exec node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/commands/doctor/shared/channel-doctor.test.ts --testNamePattern="reports throwing empty allowlist|keeps healthy preview" --reporter=verbose
OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_FS_MODULE_CACHE_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-vitest-next174-rebased nodenv exec node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/commands/doctor/shared/channel-doctor.test.ts src/cli/plugins-install-config.test.ts --reporter=verbose
OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_FS_MODULE_CACHE_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-vitest-next174-flow-rebased nodenv exec node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/commands/doctor-config-flow.test.ts --testNamePattern="emits warning-only channel stale cleanup" --reporter=verbose
nodenv exec node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs src/commands/doctor/shared/channel-doctor.ts src/commands/doctor/shared/channel-doctor.test.ts src/commands/doctor-config-flow.ts src/commands/doctor-config-flow.test.ts src/cli/plugins-install-command.ts src/cli/plugins-install-config.test.ts
git diff --check origin/main...HEAD
.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode branch --base origin/main
blacksmith testbox list --all
node scripts/crabbox-wrapper.mjs run -provider aws -label next174-check-changed -shell -- "pnpm check:changed"
```

Evidence after fix: the red repro failed pre-fix with `channel doctor extra hook exploded` and `channel doctor preview hook exploded`; after the patch, `channel-doctor.test.ts` passed 15 tests and `plugins-install-config.test.ts` passed 12 tests after rebasing onto `origin/main`.

Observed result after fix: throwing channel doctor hooks now produce sanitized warnings such as `channels.matrix: channel plugin doctor hook collectPreviewWarnings failed (...)`, healthy sibling channel warnings still appear, doctor emits stale-cleanup warning-only diagnostics, and plugin install recovery rejects stale cleanup warnings instead of silently continuing.

What was not tested: full `pnpm check:changed` did not run because Testbox auth is missing and AWS Crabbox coordinator lease creation returned 401 before remote execution.

Proof limitations or environment constraints: broad remote proof is blocked in this shell by `blacksmith testbox list --all` reporting `not authenticated` and the Crabbox coordinator returning HTTP 401 for run/lease creation.

Before evidence (optional but encouraged): the focused red repro against the old source failed before warnings could be collected with the raw plugin hook errors above.

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

- Red repro command listed above: failed pre-fix with the raw hook errors.
- Post-rebase focused file proof: `channel-doctor.test.ts` 15 tests passed; `plugins-install-config.test.ts` 12 tests passed.
- Post-rebase doctor-flow targeted proof: warning-only stale cleanup diagnostic test passed.
- `nodenv exec node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs ...`: passed.
- `git diff --check origin/main...HEAD`: passed.
- Branch autoreview: clean, no accepted/actionable findings.

What regression coverage was added or updated?

- Channel doctor hook failures for empty allowlist extra warnings, preview warnings, mutable allowlist warnings, compatibility mutations, stale cleanup, repair, and config sequences.
- Doctor config flow coverage for warning-only stale cleanup diagnostics.
- Plugin install recovery coverage for aborting when stale channel cleanup reports warnings.

What failed before this fix, if known?

- `channel-doctor.test.ts` red repro failed with `channel doctor extra hook exploded` and `channel doctor preview hook exploded`.

If no test was added, why not?

Not applicable.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes. Doctor now reports channel plugin doctor hook failures instead of crashing; plugin install recovery now aborts when stale channel cleanup reports warnings.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes, narrowly for channel doctor stale cleanup failure handling during doctor/install recovery.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No.

What is the highest-risk area?

The install recovery caller now treats stale cleanup warnings as blocking because it cannot display doctor warnings.

How is that risk mitigated?

The rejection only happens after the recovery path is already operating on invalid config and a channel stale cleanup hook reports warnings. Doctor itself surfaces those warnings so the operator can repair first.

## Current review state

What is the next action?

Review the draft PR and run broad remote changed-gate proof from an authenticated Testbox/Crabbox environment.

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?

Broad `pnpm check:changed` is waiting on Testbox or Crabbox auth.

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

Local autoreview found and verified two caller-handling issues for stale cleanup warnings; both were fixed before the final branch autoreview passed clean.

target.title: fix(secrets): scope env scrub to migrated providers' vars
target.body:
## Summary

Migrating one provider's API key to a SecretRef can silently delete a different provider's credential from `~/.openclaw/.env`. When two known-secret env vars hold the same value (for example `OPENAI_API_KEY` and `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` both pointing at one shared LiteLLM/proxy gateway key), running `openclaw secrets configure` / `apply` to migrate only one of them scrubs both `.env` lines. The non-migrated provider was never converted to a ref, so it loses its only credential and fails to authenticate on the next run.

## Root cause

The post-migration env scrub matches lines by value only, against the union of every provider's known env var names. It has no notion of which provider was actually migrated.

`src/secrets/apply.ts` (before):

```ts
const envRawByPath = scrubEnvFiles({
  env: params.env,
  scrubbedValues: targetMutations.scrubbedValues,
  changedFiles,
  enabled: options.scrubEnv,
});
```

```ts
const scrubbed = scrubEnvRaw(
  current,
  params.scrubbedValues,
  new Set(listKnownSecretEnvVarNames()),
);
```

`scrubbedValues` is a `Set<string>` of plaintext values with no env-key/provider association. `scrubEnvRaw` deletes a line when its key is in the allowed-key set AND its value is in `scrubbedValues`. Because the allowed-key set is `listKnownSecretEnvVarNames()` (all providers), two different providers sharing a value collapse to one match and both lines are removed, even though only one was migrated. `scrubEnv` defaults to `true` (`src/secrets/plan.ts:192`), so this is the default path.

## Fix

Scope the scrub to the migrated providers' env vars. Build the allowed-key set from the resolved targets and pass it to `scrubEnvFiles` instead of the global list. Crucially, only a target that migrates the provider's *primary auth credential* expands to that provider's env vars; provider-indexed non-auth targets (`models.providers.*.headers.*`, `models.providers.*.request.auth.*`, the TLS targets) share the same `providerId` but must not delete the provider's still-live auth env line.

`src/secrets/apply.ts` (after):

```ts
const knownSecretEnvKeys = new Set(listKnownSecretEnvVarNames());
const scrubbedProviderEnvKeys = new Set<string>();
for (const { resolved, target } of targetMutations.resolvedTargets) {
  const migratesProviderAuth =
    resolved.entry.trackProviderShadowing === true || resolved.entry.authProfileType != null;
  if (migratesProviderAuth) {
    const providerId = (
      resolved.providerId ??
      target.providerId ??
      target.authProfileProvider ??
      targetMutations.authProfileProviderByTarget.get(target) ??
      ""
    ).trim();
    if (providerId) {
      for (const envKey of getProviderEnvVars(normalizeProviderId(providerId))) {
        scrubbedProviderEnvKeys.add(envKey);
      }
    }
  }
  const envRefId = target.ref.source === "env" ? target.ref.id.trim() : "";
  if (envRefId && knownSecretEnvKeys.has(envRefId)) {
    scrubbedProviderEnvKeys.add(envRefId);
  }
}

const envRawByPath = scrubEnvFiles({
  env: params.env,
  scrubbedValues: targetMutations.scrubbedValues,
  scrubbedProviderEnvKeys,
  changedFiles,
  enabled: options.scrubEnv,
});
```

`scrubEnvFiles` now takes `scrubbedProviderEnvKeys` and forwards it to `scrubEnvRaw`. A `.env` line is removed only when both its key belongs to a migrated provider's auth surface and its value matches a migrated value. The migrated provider's line is still scrubbed; an unrelated provider's var sharing the same value is no longer eligible.

Two registry properties gate the provider-env expansion to actual credential targets:

- `entry.trackProviderShadowing` marks `models.providers.*.apiKey` (the provider's primary auth credential, defined in `src/secrets/target-registry-data.ts`).
- `entry.authProfileType` marks the auth-profile credential targets (`auth-profiles.api_key.key`, `auth-profiles.token.token`). Their provider identity is not carried in `providerId`; it comes from `authProfileProvider` when the plan creates a new mapping, and from the auth-profile store's `profiles.<id>.provider` for an existing profile. `applyAuthProfileTargetMutation` already loads/mutates that store, so it now returns the resolved provider and the apply loop records it in `authProfileProviderByTarget`. The env-scrub derivation falls back to that map, so an existing auth-profile target migrating to a file/exec ref (no `authProfileProvider`, no env-ref id) still expands to the provider's env vars and scrubs the matching `.env` line, matching what pristine `main` did.

A `models.providers.*.headers.*` or `request.auth.*` target resolves the same `providerId` from its path, but carries neither flag, so migrating only a provider header whose value happens to equal `OPENAI_API_KEY` no longer deletes the live `OPENAI_API_KEY` line.

Two target classes contribute migrated plaintext through the env-ref-id branch instead of provider expansion:

- Plugin/config secret targets (for example the Firecrawl `tools.web.fetch.firecrawl.apiKey` target, or bundled web-provider `plugins.entries.<id>.config.webSearch.apiKey` targets) expose neither `providerId` nor `authProfileProvider`. For every migrated target whose SecretRef is an env ref AND whose env var id is a known provider/plugin secret env key (`listKnownSecretEnvVarNames()`), the ref id is added to the allow-list, so a plugin/config-only migration still scrubs its own line (such as `FIRECRAWL_API_KEY`).
- Non-provider env-ref targets stay protected: a target like `gateway.auth.token` migrating to `{ source: "env", id: "OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_TOKEN" }` keeps its `.env` line, because `OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_TOKEN` is not a known provider/plugin secret env key and deleting it would break the very ref that now reads from it.

## Why this is the right boundary

The defect is in how the env scrub derives its allowed-key set, so the fix lives at the same call site that already has the resolved targets. The provider-to-env-var mapping reuses the existing `getProviderEnvVars` / `normalizeProviderId` helpers (the same `normalizeProviderId(resolved.providerId)` pattern already used a few lines above for `providerTargets`), and the credential gate reuses the registry's own `trackProviderShadowing` / `authProfileType` metadata rather than inventing a new classification. Sibling scrub paths are unaffected: the auth-profiles and legacy `auth.json` scrubs are already keyed per migrated provider/path, not by a global value set; only the `.env` scrub used the broad key list.

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/secrets/apply.test.ts src/secrets/apply.envscrub-collision.repro.test.ts` -> 27 passed (21 existing + 6 new). The existing "remove migrated key, keep UNRELATED" case stays green.
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs src/secrets/apply.ts src/secrets/apply.envscrub-collision.repro.test.ts` -> clean.
- `oxfmt --check src/secrets/apply.ts src/secrets/apply.envscrub-collision.repro.test.ts` -> all files correctly formatted.
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json` / `-p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json` -> no errors in the changed files.
- New test `src/secrets/apply.envscrub-collision.repro.test.ts` has six cases, all driving the real `runSecretsApply`: the shared-value collision (fails on pristine `origin/main`), an auth-profile migration that must still scrub its `.env` line (guards the `authProfileType`/`authProfileProvider` derivation), a same-value `models.providers.*.headers.*` migration that must preserve `OPENAI_API_KEY` (guards the credential gate; fails on the provider-id-only revision), a plugin/config Firecrawl migration that must scrub `FIRECRAWL_API_KEY` while preserving an unrelated same-value `OPENAI_API_KEY` (guards the env-ref-id derivation), a non-provider `gateway.auth.token` migration whose `OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_TOKEN` env source must be preserved (guards the known-secret-env gate), and an existing auth-profile target migrating to a file ref WITHOUT `authProfileProvider`, whose matching `OPENAI_API_KEY` line must still be scrubbed via the store-derived provider (guards the new fallback; fails on the provider-id-only revision, which left the key in plaintext).

Closest existing reports are unrelated: #84763 / #42250 / #56581 scrub provider env from ACP/exec child processes; #92522 is the opposite direction (audit misses LaunchAgent service-env plaintext, i.e. under-scrubbing). None cover this `.env` value-collision over-deletion.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: (1) migrating only the openai apiKey to a SecretRef must not delete `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` from `~/.openclaw/.env` when both vars share one value; (2) migrating only a same-value provider header (`models.providers.openai.headers.*`) must NOT delete `OPENAI_API_KEY`, because the provider's auth credential was not migrated; (3) migrating a plugin/config secret target (Firecrawl) must still scrub its own `FIRECRAWL_API_KEY` line while leaving an unrelated same-value provider var intact; (4) migrating a non-provider secret target (`gateway.auth.token`) must NOT delete its `OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_TOKEN` env source, since that env key is not a known provider/plugin secret env key and the ref now reads from it; (5) migrating an existing auth-profile credential to a file/exec ref (no `authProfileProvider` on the plan) must still scrub the provider's matching `OPENAI_API_KEY` line, deriving the provider from the on-disk auth-profile store, matching what pristine `main` did.
Real environment tested: drove the real `openclaw secrets apply --from <plan>` CLI command (the registered `secrets apply` action calling `runSecretsApply({ write: true })`) against a real temp state dir on disk, with `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR` pointing at a `.env` seeded per scenario, on pristine `origin/main` / the prior PR revision and on the patched tree with identical inputs. No vitest, no mocks: the CLI parsed the plan file and rewrote the on-disk `.env`.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: `openclaw secrets apply --from plan.json` (scrubEnv default true), then `cat $OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR/.env`.
Evidence after fix:
```
# BEFORE (pristine main, real CLI)
$ cat .env
OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-shared-proxy-key
ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=sk-shared-proxy-key
UNRELATED=value
$ openclaw secrets apply --from plan.json
Secrets applied. Updated 2 file(s).
$ cat .env
UNRELATED=value            # ANTHROPIC_API_KEY DELETED -> non-migrated provider loses its credential

# AFTER (this patch, real CLI, identical inputs)
$ cat .env
OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-shared-proxy-key
ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=sk-shared-proxy-key
UNRELATED=value
$ openclaw secrets apply --from plan.json
Secrets applied. Updated 2 file(s).
$ cat .env
ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=sk-shared-proxy-key   # preserved
UNRELATED=value                         # only the migrated OPENAI_API_KEY line removed
```
Same-value provider-header target (`models.providers.openai.headers.x-shared-header` -> `OPENAI_PROXY_HEADER`), `.env` with `OPENAI_API_KEY=<shared>`, `OPENAI_PROXY_HEADER=<shared>`, `UNRELATED=value`, plan migrating only the header (the provider apiKey stays a live `OPENAI_API_KEY` env ref):
```
# BEFORE (provider-id-only revision, real CLI)
$ openclaw secrets apply --from plan.json
Secrets applied.
$ cat .env
OPENAI_PROXY_HEADER=sk-shared-proxy-key
UNRELATED=value                         # OPENAI_API_KEY DELETED -> provider auth lost though only a header was migrated

# AFTER (this patch, real CLI, identical inputs)
$ openclaw secrets apply --from plan.json
Secrets applied.
$ cat .env
OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-shared-proxy-key      # preserved: header migration does not shadow provider auth
OPENAI_PROXY_HEADER=sk-shared-proxy-key
UNRELATED=value
```
Plugin/config target (Firecrawl) via the same real CLI, `.env` with `FIRECRAWL_API_KEY=<shared>`, `OPENAI_API_KEY=<shared>`, `UNRELATED=value`, plan migrating only `tools.web.fetch.firecrawl.apiKey`:
```
# BEFORE (provider-id-only revision, real CLI)
$ openclaw secrets apply --from plan.json
Secrets applied. Updated 1 file(s).
$ cat .env
FIRECRAWL_API_KEY=sk-shared-proxy-key   # NOT scrubbed -> migrated plugin credential left in plaintext
OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-shared-proxy-key
UNRELATED=value

# AFTER (this patch, real CLI, identical inputs)
$ openclaw secrets apply --from plan.json
Secrets applied. Updated 2 file(s).
$ cat .env
OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-shared-proxy-key      # unrelated same-value provider preserved
UNRELATED=value                         # only the migrated FIRECRAWL_API_KEY line removed
```
Non-provider env-ref target (`gateway.auth.token` -> `OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_TOKEN`) via the same real CLI, `.env` with `OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_TOKEN=<shared>`, `UNRELATED=value`:
```
# BEFORE (ungated ref-id revision, real CLI)
$ openclaw secrets apply --from plan.json
Secrets applied. Updated 2 file(s).
$ cat .env
UNRELATED=value                         # OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_TOKEN DELETED -> ref source broken, next runtime resolution fails

# AFTER (this patch, real CLI, identical inputs)
$ openclaw secrets apply --from plan.json
Secrets applied. Updated 1 file(s).
$ cat .env
OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_TOKEN=sk-shared-proxy-key   # preserved: not a known secret env key, env source kept
UNRELATED=value
```
Existing auth-profile target migrating to a file ref WITHOUT `authProfileProvider` (provider known only from the on-disk auth-profile store at `profiles.openai:default.provider`), driven on the real production `runSecretsApply` (the same function the `secrets apply` CLI action calls; no vitest, no mocks; the per-agent auth-profile SQLite store seeded via `saveAuthProfileStore`) on the prior provider-id-only revision vs this patch, `.env` with `OPENAI_API_KEY=<shared>`, `UNRELATED=value`, plan migrating only `profiles.openai:default.key` to `{ source: "file", provider: "mounted", id: "value" }`:
```
# BEFORE (provider-id-only revision, real runtime, identical inputs)
$ openclaw secrets apply --from plan.json
Secrets applied. Updated 1 file(s).
$ cat .env
OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-shared-proxy-key      # NOT scrubbed -> provider env key left in plaintext though pristine main would remove it
UNRELATED=value

# AFTER (this patch, real runtime, identical inputs)
$ openclaw secrets apply --from plan.json
Secrets applied. Updated 2 file(s).
$ cat .env
UNRELATED=value                         # OPENAI_API_KEY removed via the store-derived provider; auth-profile store + .env both updated
```
Observed result after fix: the migrated line is removed only for an actually-migrated provider/plugin auth credential (openai apiKey in scenario 1, firecrawl in scenario 3, the auth-profile credential in scenario 5); a non-migrated same-value provider var survives whether the same-value sibling is another provider's key (scenario 1), a header on the same provider (scenario 2), or a plugin credential (scenario 3); a non-provider env-ref source (`OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_TOKEN`, scenario 4) is preserved; and an existing auth-profile credential migrating to a non-env ref still scrubs its provider's `.env` line via the store-derived provider (scenario 5), closing the under-scrub regression the prior revision introduced.
What was not tested: the literal compiled `openclaw` CLI binary was not driven for these scenarios because a full local build OOMs on this box; scenarios 1-4 ran through the registered CLI `secrets apply` action and scenario 5 through the same `runSecretsApply` production function it calls, both on real on-disk state with no mocks. The legacy `auth.json` and auth-profiles store scrubs were not re-driven because they are already keyed per migrated provider and were not changed.

target.title: fix(cron): drop unenforceable default toolsAllow cap on CLI-backed runs
target.body:
## Problem

#91499 auto-stamps the creator tool surface onto every `agentTurn` cron payload as a `toolsAllow` cap. CLI backends cannot enforce a runtime `toolsAllow`, and `cli-runner/prepare.ts` throws on any defined `toolsAllow`, so every scheduled `agentTurn` that resolves to a CLI backend (e.g. `claude-cli`) fails to start. This silently broke all per-thread scheduled continuations on CLI backends.

## Fix

Decide where the backend is actually known — at run time — instead of predicting it at create time:

- **Flag the auto-applied cap.** `capCronAgentTurnToolsAllow` stamps `toolsAllowIsDefault` when it fills in the full creator surface (no explicit allow-list, or a bare `"*"`). An explicit narrowing or empty allow-list is a real restriction and carries no flag.
- **Drop it at run time.** The cron run-executor already branches on the resolved provider; in the CLI branch it now drops a flagged default cap (unenforceable there) and lets the run proceed, while an explicit restriction (no flag) is still passed through so `prepare.ts` fails closed and surfaces *"use an embedded runtime for restricted tool policy"*. Embedded runs are untouched and keep the full cap.
- **Persist the flag.** New nullable `cron_jobs.payload_tools_allow_is_default` column (additive `ensureColumn` migration + codec read/write) so the decision survives a gateway restart; without it a reloaded job would re-hit the rejection.

This removes the create-time provider-resolution heuristic entirely, so the CLI-vs-embedded decision can no longer diverge from what the run resolves.

## Scope boundary

- Embedded runs are unchanged and keep the full creator-surface cap.
- Only the *auto-applied default* cap is dropped on CLI; an explicit/narrowed/empty `toolsAllow` is still enforced (fails closed via `prepare.ts`).

## Verification

- `tsgo:core`: clean (exit 0)
- `vitest` cron + store + isolated-agent suites: 681 passing on current `main` base
- `oxlint` on changed files: clean
- run-executor drops the flagged default but propagates an explicit restriction on CLI; cron-tool stamps/clears the flag across create and update paths; store round-trips the flag (and its absence) through SQLite

## Risk / mitigation

- **Risk:** a reloaded job loses the in-memory flag and re-hits the rejection. **Mitigation:** the flag is persisted in SQLite via the additive column and round-tripped by the codec.
- **Not covered:** `agentTurn` crons created during the regression window keep a flagless `toolsAllow` until next re-saved (any update re-stamps them); they remain fail-closed on CLI until then.

commands.author: mmyzwl

target.title: fix(agent-runner): classify transient provider errors as fallback-worthy
target.body:
## Summary

When a primary provider returns an `upstream_error` / `HTTP 500` / transient server error, the embedded agent run result classifier discards it (returns null), so `runWithModelFallback` never advances to the next configured fallback model. The turn ends immediately with `LLM request failed` even when healthy fallback models are configured and ready.

Fixes #95519

## Changes

- **`result-fallback-classifier.ts`**: Renamed `classifyBusinessDenialErrorPayloadReason` → `classifyProviderErrorPayloadReason` and extended the switch statement to also pass through `server_error` and `timeout` failover reasons (previously only `auth`/`auth_permanent`/`billing` were passed through).
- **`result-fallback-classifier.test.ts`**: Updated the "does not retry non-business transport error payloads" test case to expect a proper fallback classification for `HTTP 500: internal server error` instead of null.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed**: Provider transient errors (`upstream_error`, `HTTP 500`, server timeout) now correctly trigger configured model fallbacks instead of ending the turn immediately.

**Evidence type**: terminal

**Real setup tested**: Node v24.13.1, Linux x86_64, worktree branch on openclaw/openclaw@main

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```bash
node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/result-fallback-classifier.test.ts
node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/agents/outcome-fallback-runtime-contract.test.ts
node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/agents/model-fallback.test.ts
```

**After-fix evidence**:

```
$ CI=true pnpm test -- src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/result-fallback-classifier.test.ts

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  11 passed (11)

$ CI=true pnpm test -- src/agents/outcome-fallback-runtime-contract.test.ts

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  10 passed (10)

$ CI=true pnpm test -- src/agents/model-fallback.test.ts

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  82 passed (82)
```

**Observed result after the fix**: All 103 tests pass across 3 related test suites. The classifier now correctly identifies `server_error` and `timeout` provider errors as fallback-worthy alongside the existing `auth`/`auth_permanent`/`billing` classifications.

**What was not tested**: Live end-to-end verification with a real provider returning `upstream_error` was not performed. The fix is validated at the unit-test layer which directly covers the classification code path.

## Tests and validation

| Test Type | Result | Details |
|-----------|--------|---------|
| result-fallback-classifier | ✅ | 11/11 passed |
| outcome-fallback-runtime-contract | ✅ | 10/10 passed |
| model-fallback | ✅ | 82/82 passed |

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes` / `No`)

**Yes** — users with configured fallback models will now see automatic fallback on transient provider errors (`upstream_error`, `HTTP 500`, timeouts) instead of immediate turn failure. This is the intended fix.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes` / `No`)

**No**

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes` / `No`)

**No** — only the internal fallback classification path is changed; auth/auth_permanent errors already triggered fallback before this change.

What is the highest-risk area?
- Over-fallbacking if the classifier is too broad

How is that risk mitigated?
- Only `server_error` and `timeout` (two specific transient errors already identified by the existing `classifyFailoverReason` function) are added. All other classifications (format, rate_limit, overloaded, model_not_found, etc.) remain filtered out as before. The non-GPT model fallthrough path is unchanged.

## Current review state

What is the next action?
- Maintainer review

PR surface: Source +12, Tests +358, Docs +8. Total +378 across 5 files.

target.title: fix(skills): route installs to requested agent workspace [AI-assisted]
target.body:
## Summary

What problem does this PR solve?

- Routes workspace-affecting skill install/update calls through the existing agent workspace resolver when an explicit `agentId` is provided.
- Keeps default behavior unchanged for callers that do not pass an `agentId`.
- Documents the new workspace selector in the Gateway protocol docs.
- Adds focused regression coverage and a real local Gateway upload-install proof for explicit agent workspaces.

Why does this matter now?

- Issue #89743 reports skill loading/install behavior using the fallback workspace instead of the configured workspace.

What is the intended outcome?

- Skill install and ClawHub update operations can target the selected agent workspace instead of always resolving from the default agent.

What is intentionally out of scope?

- No changes to skill loading semantics, workspace config defaults, selected-agent UI pass-through, or unrelated skill APIs.

What does success look like?

- Existing callers keep their current behavior, while callers that provide `agentId` get install/update operations routed to that agent workspace.

What should reviewers focus on?

- Whether `agentId` is the intended public Gateway selector for skill mutations.
- Whether selected-agent UI pass-through should be part of this PR or tracked separately.

## Linked context

Which issue does this close?

Related #89743

Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related?

This PR addresses the backend Gateway routing gap for skill install/update. The linked user-facing issue may still need UI/client follow-through if maintainers expect Control UI install calls to pass `agentId`.

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

No maintainer-specific implementation direction yet. I commented on the issue before opening this PR.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior addressed: skill install/update operations could resolve the default agent workspace instead of an explicit/custom agent workspace.
- Real environment tested: Windows 11 local checkout, bundled Node v24.14.0 from the Codex runtime, temporary OpenClaw state/config/workspace directories, and real Gateway upload/install handlers.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - `& 'C:\Users\aliab\.cache\codex-runtimes\codex-primary-runtime\dependencies\node\bin\node.exe' --import tsx scripts\repro\issue-89743-skill-agent-workspace-proof.mts`
  - `& 'C:\Users\aliab\.cache\codex-runtimes\codex-primary-runtime\dependencies\node\bin\node.exe' scripts\run-vitest.mjs exec node node_modules/vitest/vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts src/gateway/server-methods/skills-upload-agent-workspace.test.ts`
  - `& 'C:\Users\aliab\.cache\codex-runtimes\codex-primary-runtime\dependencies\node\bin\node.exe' scripts\run-vitest.mjs exec node node_modules/vitest/vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts src/gateway/server-methods/skills.clawhub.test.ts`
  - `& 'C:\Users\aliab\.cache\codex-runtimes\codex-primary-runtime\dependencies\node\bin\node.exe' scripts\run-vitest.mjs exec node node_modules/vitest/vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts src/gateway/server-methods/skills-upload.test.ts`
  - `git diff --check`
- Evidence after fix:
  - Console output from the local Gateway proof command, with temp paths redacted:
    ```text
    Extracting <temp>\state\tmp\skill-uploads\<upload-id>\archive.zip...
    Installing to <temp>\workspace-writer\skills\proof-upload...
    REAL_GATEWAY_PROOF issue=89743
    flow=skills.upload.begin/chunk/commit -> skills.install source=upload agentId=writer
    install.ok=true
    targetDir=<temp>\workspace-writer\skills\proof-upload
    writerSkillExists=true
    mainSkillExists=false
    writerSkillContainsProofUpload=true
    ```
  - `src/gateway/server-methods/skills-upload-agent-workspace.test.ts`: `Test Files 1 passed (1)`, `Tests 1 passed (1)`.
  - `src/gateway/server-methods/skills.clawhub.test.ts`: `Test Files 1 passed (1)`, `Tests 11 passed (11)`.
  - `src/gateway/server-methods/skills-upload.test.ts`: `Test Files 1 passed (1)`, `Tests 10 passed (10)`.
  - `git diff --check` completed with no output.
- Observed result after fix: focused regression tests verify ClawHub install, local dependency install, and ClawHub update use the explicit `writer` agent workspace when `agentId: "writer"` is supplied.
- The real upload-install proof creates temporary `main` and `writer` workspaces, uploads a zip archive through `skills.upload.*`, calls `skills.install` with `agentId: "writer"`, verifies `targetDir` is the writer workspace, verifies `SKILL.md` exists there, and verifies the same skill is absent from the main workspace.
- What was not tested: a full packaged OpenClaw desktop/manual UI workflow or Control UI selected-agent pass-through.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: `pnpm docs:list` and `corepack pnpm install --frozen-lockfile` were blocked by dependency reconciliation/network fetch failures in this Codex worktree, but enough dependencies were present to run the focused Gateway tests above. `codex review --base origin/main` was attempted earlier and failed locally with Windows `Access is denied` for `codex.exe`.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): the previous code resolved install/update workspace paths from `resolveDefaultAgentId(cfg)` instead of the request context.

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

- `& 'C:\Users\aliab\.cache\codex-runtimes\codex-primary-runtime\dependencies\node\bin\node.exe' scripts\run-vitest.mjs exec node node_modules/vitest/vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts src/gateway/server-methods/skills-upload-agent-workspace.test.ts`
- `& 'C:\Users\aliab\.cache\codex-runtimes\codex-primary-runtime\dependencies\node\bin\node.exe' scripts\run-vitest.mjs exec node node_modules/vitest/vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts src/gateway/server-methods/skills.clawhub.test.ts`
- `& 'C:\Users\aliab\.cache\codex-runtimes\codex-primary-runtime\dependencies\node\bin\node.exe' scripts\run-vitest.mjs exec node node_modules/vitest/vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts src/gateway/server-methods/skills-upload.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`

What regression coverage was added or updated?

- Added coverage for ClawHub install into an explicit agent workspace.
- Added coverage for local skill dependency install into an explicit agent workspace.
- Added coverage for ClawHub update in an explicit agent workspace.
- Added a real upload-install Gateway proof that installs an uploaded archive into an explicit `writer` agent workspace and verifies it does not land in the default `main` workspace.

What failed before this fix, if known?

- The new explicit-workspace regression expectations fail against the old routing because install/update used the default agent workspace resolver.

If no test was added, why not?

- Tests were added.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes, for callers that pass the new optional `agentId` field to skill install/update APIs.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No.

What is the highest-risk area?

- Gateway API compatibility and whether install/update callers should select workspaces via `agentId`.

How is that risk mitigated?

- The new schema field is optional.
- Existing no-`agentId` behavior falls back through the existing resolver.
- The implementation reuses `resolveSkillsAgentWorkspace`, which nearby skills APIs already use for agent workspace selection.
- Gateway protocol docs now describe the selector and fallback/rejection behavior.

## Current review state

What is the next action?

- Waiting for Ali review before marking ready.

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?

- Waiting for CI and maintainer review.
- Ali should review/understand the patch before the PR is marked ready.
- Maintainers should decide whether selected-agent UI/client pass-through belongs in this PR or a follow-up.

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

- Addressed ClawSweeper's docs finding by updating `docs/gateway/protocol.md`.
- Added real Gateway upload-install behavior proof for explicit agent workspace routing.

AI disclosure:

- This PR was prepared with AI assistance from Codex. I reviewed the diff and ran the focused validation commands listed above.

target.title: fix(agents): quarantine invalid bundle MCP tools
target.body:
## Summary
- Request bundled MCP `tools/list` with a permissive outer schema, then validate tools one by one.
- Quarantine invalid MCP tool entries and SDK metadata-cache failures with catalog diagnostics while keeping healthy sibling tools available.
- Preserve SDK metadata caching for surviving tools, including paginated lists and empty/all-invalid refreshes, so output-schema validation and task-support behavior remain intact.

## Verification
- `./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --write src/agents/agent-bundle-mcp-runtime.ts src/agents/agent-bundle-mcp-runtime.test.ts`
- `./node_modules/.bin/oxlint src/agents/agent-bundle-mcp-runtime.ts src/agents/agent-bundle-mcp-runtime.test.ts`
- `CI=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/agents/agent-bundle-mcp-runtime.test.ts -t "keeps active MCP sessions usable when catalog refresh records diagnostics|rebuilds metadata cache for surviving tools after paginated quarantine|skips tools whose output schema metadata cannot be cached" --reporter=verbose`
- `CI=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/agents/agent-bundle-mcp-runtime.test.ts --reporter=verbose`
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode branch --base origin/main`
- AWS Crabbox `run_1f1dd0c0fc88`, lease `cbx_93cd7d890628`, type `c7a.8xlarge`: `node scripts/crabbox-wrapper.mjs run --provider aws --idle-timeout 90m --ttl 240m --timing-json -- env OPENCLAW_CHECK_CHANGED_REMOTE_CHILD=1 OPENCLAW_CHANGED_LANES_RAW_SYNC=1 corepack pnpm check:changed`

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: A bundled MCP server could return one invalid `tools/list` entry and cause OpenClaw to reject the whole list, dropping healthy sibling tools and reporting only a server-level startup diagnostic.

Real environment tested: Local Codex worktree on branch `fuzz-tool-schema-next66-20260603`, Node/Vitest through `scripts/run-vitest.mjs`; AWS Crabbox `run_1f1dd0c0fc88` / `cbx_93cd7d890628`, Linux `c7a.8xlarge`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: See Verification above.

Evidence after fix: The pre-patch regression failed with `catalog.tools` equal to `[]` instead of `healthy_tool`. After the patch, focused MCP tests passed for invalid input-schema quarantine, invalid output-metadata quarantine, paginated metadata-cache preservation, and all-invalid refresh cache clearing. Full `agent-bundle-mcp-runtime.test.ts` passed: 1 file, 33 tests. Autoreview reported no accepted/actionable findings. Remote `pnpm check:changed` selected lanes `core, coreTests` and exited 0.

Observed result after fix: Invalid MCP tools are skipped with diagnostics naming the offending tool; healthy sibling tools remain in the catalog; surviving tools are re-cached through the SDK metadata path; stale metadata is cleared when a refresh leaves no surviving tools.

What was not tested: Full repo suite was not run; `pnpm check:changed` passed remotely for the touched lanes.

target.title: fix(tavily): fall back to process.env when config SecretRef is unresolvable
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`tavily_search` and `tavily_extract` fail at runtime with `unresolved SecretRef "env:default:TAVILY_API_KEY"` even when `TAVILY_API_KEY` is set in the gateway process environment. The configured `SecretRef` points at an env-backed credential that the active runtime snapshot can't inline-resolve, but the plugin's own call chain already has a `process.env.TAVILY_API_KEY` fallback — `normalizeConfiguredSecret` in strict mode throws before that fallback can run. Bundled peers (`brave`, `firecrawl`, `exa`) use `readConfiguredSecretString` from `extensions/web-search-provider-common` and don't hit this; tavily and exa are the only remaining `extensions/*/src/config.ts` helpers still throwing in strict mode.

## Evidence

**Environment:** Linux, Node v24, OpenClaw `v2026.6.8` (commit `8a24f93`), with the patch applied via `prestart.sh` step 7/7 in `the contributor's runtime overlay`.

**Reproduction (before fix):**

```bash
export TAVILY_API_KEY=***
openclaw config set plugins.entries.tavily.config.webSearch.apiKey \
  --ref-provider default --ref-source env --ref-id TAVILY_API_KEY
openclaw infer web search --provider tavily --query ping --limit 1 --json
# → Error: plugins.entries.tavily.config.webSearch.apiKey: unresolved SecretRef "env:default:TAVILY_API_KEY". Resolve this command against an active gateway runtime snapshot before reading it.
```

**After this patch:** Same command reaches Tavily. With a dummy key, the expected downstream error is `401 Unauthorized: missing or invalid API key` (auth failure, not SecretRef failure). With a live key, the call returns search results. Observed round-trip ~1.3s for `tavily_search`.

**Test coverage added (`extensions/tavily/src/config.test.ts`):**

- returns the configured string `apiKey` as-is (happy path)
- falls back to `process.env.TAVILY_API_KEY` when the configured env SecretRef is unresolvable AND its id is `TAVILY_API_KEY`
- does **NOT** fall back when the configured SecretRef is a file source (regression coverage for the silent-override scenario)
- does **NOT** fall back when the configured env SecretRef targets a different env var (regression coverage)
- returns `undefined` when neither config nor env var is set

**What was not tested:** A clean from-source `pnpm test` from a fresh checkout — patch was validated against a compiled `dist/` of `v2026.6.8` and a manually-patched version of the same file. CI re-validates from source on every push.

## Real behavior proof (2026-06-21, isolated test pod on k3s `a private k3s cluster`)

Re-ran a clean BEFORE/AFTER against a fresh test pod with the
the runtime sed-patch **deliberately disabled** so the
upstream unpatched dist file is exercised. Config used the same
`{source: env, provider: default, id: TAVILY_API_KEY}` shape that
production ships in `openclaw.json`.

### BEFORE — unpatched dist

Synthetic direct-module call (Node ESM import of the bundled
`tavily-client-Dh_YnIM9.js`):

```
$ node ./repro.mjs

=== ERROR (no fix) ===
name=UnresolvedSecretInputError
message=plugins.entries.tavily.config.webSearch.apiKey: unresolved SecretRef "env:default:TAVILY_API_KEY". Resolve this command against an active gateway runtime snapshot before reading it.
path="plugins.entries.tavily.config.webSearch.apiKey"
```

Real LLM-driven MCP tool calls captured from the test pod's gateway log
on 2026-06-21T12:05:16Z, when an agent tried a research query and
the MCP `tavily_search` / `tavily_extract` tools failed for the same
reason:

```
2026-06-21T12:05:16.450+00:00 [tools] tavily_search failed: plugins.entries.tavily.config.webSearch.apiKey: unresolved SecretRef "env:default:TAVILY_API_KEY". Resolve this command against an active gateway runtime snapshot before reading it. raw_params={"query":"[research query]","max_results":10,"include_answer":true}
2026-06-21T12:05:16.462+00:00 [tools] tavily_search failed: plugins.entries.tavily.config.webSearch.apiKey: unresolved SecretRef "env:default:TAVILY_API_KEY". Resolve this command against an active gateway runtime snapshot before reading it. raw_params={"query":"[research query]","max_results":8,"include_answer":true}
2026-06-21T12:05:16.549+00:00 [tools] tavily_search failed: plugins.entries.tavily.config.webSearch.apiKey: unresolved SecretRef "env:default:TAVILY_API_KEY". Resolve this command against an active gateway runtime snapshot before reading it. raw_params={"query":"[research query]","max_results":8,"include_answer":true}
2026-06-21T12:05:16.553+00:00 [tools] tavily_search failed: plugins.entries.tavily.config.webSearch.apiKey: unresolved SecretRef "env:default:TAVILY_API_KEY". Resolve this command against an active gateway runtime snapshot before reading it. raw_params={"query":"[research query]","max_results":6}
2026-06-21T12:05:29.366+00:00 [tools] tavily_extract failed: plugins.entries.tavily.config.webSearch.apiKey: unresolved SecretRef "env:default:TAVILY_API_KEY". Resolve this command against an active gateway runtime snapshot before reading it. raw_params={"urls":["[redacted]"],"query":"[research query]","chunks_per_source":3}
```

This matches the symptom described in the original Evidence section
verbatim, on a freshly built `2026.6.8` image with **no runtime
patch** applied.

### AFTER — patched dist

Same test pod, same Node script, after re-patching
`/app/dist/tavily-client-Dh_YnIM9.js` in place with the same 6-line
try/catch change that PR #95110 adds to source (and that the
the runtime sed-patch applies to dist):

```
$ node ./repro.mjs

=== OK (fix active) ===
{
  "ok": true,
  "tookMs": 1099,
  "count": 1,
  "first_title": "Ping (networking utility) - Wikipedia",
  "first_url": "https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ping_(networking_utility)"
}
```

The patched `normalizeConfiguredSecret` swallows the strict-mode
`UnresolvedSecretInputError` and returns `""`, which lets
`resolveTavilyApiKey`'s `|| normalizeSecretInput(process.env.TAVILY_API_KEY)`
fallback rescue the key from the gateway's own environment.

### Dist diff (the exact change)

```diff
 function normalizeConfiguredSecret(value, path) {
-	return normalizeSecretInput(normalizeResolvedSecretInputString({
-		value,
-		path
-	}));
+	try {
+		return normalizeSecretInput(normalizeResolvedSecretInputString({
+			value,
+			path
+		}));
+	} catch {
+		// SecretRef could not be resolved against the active runtime snapshot
+		// (strict mode throws). Fall through to the process.env chain so the
+		// caller's `|| normalizeSecretInput(process.env.TAVILY_API_KEY)` can
+		// still pick up the key. Matches the pattern used by brave/firecrawl.
+		return "";
+	}
 }
```

### md5 reference

| State | md5 of `/app/dist/tavily-client-Dh_YnIM9.js` |
|---|---|
| Unpatched (upstream dist, bug present) | `[redacted md5]` |
| Patched (matches production) | `[redacted md5]` |

The production pod currently sits on the patched md5 because
the runtime re-runs the sed-patch in `prestart.sh` on every container
start. The test pod ran on the unpatched md5 to expose the original
failure mode.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix

## Scope

- [x] Integrations (tavily web-search plugin)

## Linked Issue

Fixes a regression where the bundled tavily plugin (added in PR #49200) cannot fall back to `TAVILY_API_KEY` when the configured `plugins.entries.tavily.config.webSearch.apiKey` is a SecretRef the active runtime snapshot doesn't inline-resolve. Related closed issues that did not catch this runtime-side gap:

- #76491 — closed as "implemented on current main", but the vendored tavily/exa helpers (`extensions/tavily/src/config.ts`, `extensions/exa/src/config.ts`) still throw in strict mode.
- #82621 — closed by PR #82798 for the CLI `infer web search` path; this PR fixes the runtime plugin path that #82798 left open.

## Approach (v2, replaces earlier try/catch)

Replaces the previous `try/catch` approach with the inspect-mode pattern already used in `extensions/firecrawl/src/config.ts`:

1. Calls `resolveSecretInputString` with `mode: "inspect"` so unresolved SecretRefs return `configured_unavailable` instead of throwing.

The vendored tavily plugin's `normalizeConfiguredSecret` (in `extensions/tavily/src/config.ts`) called `normalizeResolvedSecretInputString({ value, path })` directly. With `resolveSecretInputString`'s default `mode: "strict"`, this throws `UnresolvedSecretInputError` when the active runtime snapshot cannot inline-resolve the SecretRef. The throw escaped before the caller's `|| normalizeSecretInput(process.env.TAVILY_API_KEY)` chain ran.

The companion helper `readConfiguredSecretString` in `extensions/web-search-provider-common/src/web-search-provider-common.ts` (used by `brave`, `firecrawl`, `exa`, and other bundled providers) wraps the same call in a function-local error path that returns `undefined` on the same failure. That mismatch is why brave/firecrawl/exa work and tavily does not.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? **Yes** — behavior change is limited to the previously-broken unresolvable-env-ref case; success path is identical.
- Config/env changes? **No**
- Migration needed? **No**

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk:** a stricter upstream caller relies on `normalizeConfiguredSecret` throwing so it can surface the error to the user.
  - **Mitigation:** the only caller in this file (`resolveTavilyApiKey`) already has `process.env` and downstream-string fallbacks. The `||` chain in `resolveTavilyApiKey` was always meant to catch a `""` return from `normalizeConfiguredSecret`; the throw was incidental.
- **Risk:** a future plugin re-introduces the same throw pattern.
  - **Mitigation:** the new test in `extensions/tavily/src/config.test.ts` asserts the no-throw behavior, so any regression is caught at unit-test time.
- **Risk:** a future user configures a non-`TAVILY_API_KEY` env SecretRef (e.g. `OPENAI_API_KEY` for tavily) and the ambient `process.env.TAVILY_API_KEY` is silently used.
  - **Mitigation:** the new helper treats such refs as `blocked` and explicitly returns `undefined`; the new test pins this behavior.

* * *

## Generated with assistance from Aiko (AikoOpenClawBot), an AI agent running on OpenClaw 2026.6.8

- **Model:** `minimax/minimax-m3` via Kilo Gateway
- **Provider:** Kilo Gateway (`https://api.kilo.ai/api/gateway`)
- **Author:** Karol Zalewski [karol.zalewski@4zal.net](mailto:karol.zalewski@4zal.net)
- **Local reproduction:**
  - Patch + script: `[redacted local fork commit]`
  - Runtime SHA-baselined application: `[redacted runtime SHA]`
  - Isolated test pod reproduction (2026-06-21): `[redacted local artifact path]`


---

## Re-review update (2026-06-21)

ClawSweeper re-reviewed this PR at 21:12 UTC and raised the readiness
to **🦪 silver shellfish overall / 🦐 gold shrimp patch quality** (from
🧂 unranked krab on both). The two remaining rank-up items in the
2026-06-21 review are addressed in this update.

### 1. `vi.mock` hoisting fix — commit `b0ece64`

**ClawSweeper P1 (2026-06-21 21:12):** "Replace the per-test `vi.mock`
calls with real SecretRef inputs or non-hoisted dynamic mocking."

**Approach taken — real SecretRef inputs.** The bundled SDK v2026.6.8
already supports `mode: "inspect"` on `resolveSecretInputString` (see
`dist/types.secrets-*.js`, `(params.mode ?? "strict") === "strict" throw...`),
so the production resolver path runs end-to-end against the real SDK
with no `vi.mock` at all. The per-test `vi.mock(...)` factories
(which Vitest hoists to the top of the file and so applied
simultaneously rather than per-test) are removed. The file shrinks
from 167 lines to 121; the five original test cases are preserved
unchanged. Empirical probe of the new resolver against the real SDK:

```
[1. happy path (string)]                              RESULT: conf***-key
[2. env SecretRef resolves via env]                   RESULT: env-***-key
[3. file SecretRef blocked]                           RESULT: undefined
[4. wrong env var blocked]                            RESULT: undefined
[5. empty config, no env]                             RESULT: undefined
```

### 2. Runtime proof — BEFORE vs AFTER v1 (current runtime)

**ClawSweeper P1 (2026-06-21 21:12):** "Add redacted current-head
terminal or log proof showing the env SecretRef path reaches Tavily
auth failure or results."

BEFORE (unpatched dist, strict mode):

```
await runTavilySearch({cfg, query: "probe", ...});
THREW: UnresolvedSecretInputError
MESSAGE: plugins.entries.tavily.config.webSearch.apiKey:
         unresolved SecretRef "env:default:TAVILY_API_KEY".
```

AFTER v1 (current runtime, try/catch sed-patch):

```
await runTavilySearch({cfg, query: "probe", ...});
THREW: Error
MESSAGE: Tavily Search API error (401):
         {"detail":{"error":"Unauthorized: missing or invalid API key."}}
```

The `401` is from Tavily, not from OpenClaw — the request reached
Tavily and was rejected by the synthetic test key. The SecretRef was
resolved via the try/catch fallback to `process.env.TAVILY_API_KEY`
and the request was sent. This is exactly the "success path" behavior
for the v1 try/catch fix.

The full inspect-mode runtime of `98d8d8` (v2) requires the dist to be
rebuilt from current source; the direct-module probe in section 1
above exercises the same resolver against the bundled dist's
`resolveSecretInputString` and shows all five cases pass.

### 3. Static comparison: v1 try/catch vs v2 inspect-mode (98d8d8)

v1 (current sed-patch) catches **any** error and falls through, so a
file or wrong-env SecretRef is silently replaced with ambient
`process.env.TAVILY_API_KEY`:

```js
function normalizeConfiguredSecret(value, path) {
  try {
    return normalizeSecretInput(normalizeResolvedSecretInputString({value, path}));
  } catch {
    return "";  // file/exec/wrong-env ALSO fall through to process.env
  }
}
```

v2 (`98d8d8` `config.ts`) keeps the env fallback for
`env:default:TAVILY_API_KEY` only, and explicitly **blocks** every
other case:

```js
function resolveConfiguredSecret(value, path, cfg) {
  const resolved = resolveSecretInputString({value, path, defaults: cfg?.secrets?.defaults, mode: "inspect"});
  if (resolved.status === "available") return {status: "available", value: normalizeSecretInput(resolved.value)};
  if (resolved.status === "missing") return {status: "missing"};
  if (resolved.ref.source !== "env") return {status: "blocked"};     // file, exec blocked
  if (resolved.ref.id.trim() !== "TAVILY_API_KEY") return {status: "blocked"};  // wrong env id blocked
  if (!canResolveEnvSecretRefInReadOnlyPath({cfg, provider: resolved.ref.provider, id: resolved.ref.id})) {
    return {status: "blocked"};  // provider not in allowlist blocked
  }
  return normalizeSecretInput(process.env[resolved.ref.id])
    ? {status: "available", value: normalizeSecretInput(process.env[resolved.ref.id])}
    : {status: "missing"};
}
```

The v2 helper is the same pattern that
`extensions/firecrawl/src/config.ts` uses (sibling provider pattern
noted by ClawSweeper in both reviews), and adds the provider-allowlist
check on top.

### 4. Note on the `check-docs` CI failure (run 27917174363)

The CI run for commit `98d8d8` reports `check-docs: failure`. The
`check-docs` job runs `pnpm check:docs` over the merged tree of
openclaw docs and the externally-checked-out `openclaw/clawhub`
docs (see `.github/workflows/ci.yml` "Checkout ClawHub docs source"
step). This job does not inspect `extensions/tavily/src/`.

During the same 21:25–21:36 UTC window, CI runs for
`openclaw/openclaw: main` reported the **same** `check-docs`
failure (`27918039763` 21:29 UTC, `27918137564` 21:32 UTC). The
latest `main` run `27918239084` at 21:36 UTC is `success` again,
indicating the flake resolved itself. This is an external-dependency
flake, not a regression in this PR; ClawSweeper did not flag
`check-docs` in either review.

### 5. CI status on `b0ece64` (this commit)

- `Real behavior proof`, `security-sensitive-guard`, `dependency-guard`,
  `no-tabs`, `actionlint`, `Detect iOS scan scope`, `dispatch`,
  `security-sensitive-guard-detect`, `dependency-guard-detect`: success
- `preflight`, `security-fast`, `Select Critical Quality shards`,
  `Scan changed paths (precise)`: queued / in-progress at the time of
  this push. These are the same jobs that were capacity-stalled on
  the prior pushes; they do not gate ClawSweeper review.


---

## Current-head source-build proof (2026-06-21)

ClawSweeper P1 (2026-06-21 21:58 UTC): "Add redacted current-head
terminal or log proof showing the env SecretRef path reaches Tavily
auth failure or results."

The `98d8d8` PR head was built from source on the contributor's
checkout, then `extensions/tavily/src/config.test.ts` was run under
the same Vitest 4.1.8 runner the rest of the project uses
(`scripts/run-vitest.mjs` against

```

$ pnpm install --frozen-lockfile                # 1209 packages, warm cache
Done in 16.7s

RUN v4.1.8 /…/[local checkout]

Test Files  1 passed (1)
```

target.title: fix(ui): change silent cron quick-create sessionTarget to isolated
target.body:
## Summary

The "Silent" delivery preset in the Cron quick-create dialog (`cron-quick-create.ts`) maps to `sessionTarget: "main"`, but the server-side validation in `assertSupportedJobSpec()` rejects `sessionTarget="main"` combined with `payloadKind="agentTurn"` (only `"systemEvent"` payloads are allowed in main sessions). Since the wizard always uses `agentTurn` payloads, the "Silent" option silently fails — `addCronJob` throws on the server, the error is swallowed by `withCronBusy`, and the dialog remains open with no visible feedback to the user.

The fix changes `sessionTarget` from `"main"` to `"isolated"` for the "Silent" preset. The `deliveryMode: "none"` already ensures no result is announced, preserving the silent semantics. The "Notify me" and "Independent session" presets are unaffected because they already use `sessionTarget: "isolated"`.

Fixes #95073

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed**: Silent cron quick-create preset now successfully creates jobs instead of failing silently due to server-side sessionTarget validation

**Real environment tested**: Ubuntu 24.04, Node.js v24, pnpm 11.2.2, OpenClaw 2026.6.1 (upstream main aa479ac7d8)

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
```bash
pnpm test ui/src/ui/views/cron-quick-create.node.test.ts
pnpm test ui/src/ui/views/
```

**After-fix evidence**:
```
$ pnpm test ui/src/ui/views/cron-quick-create.node.test.ts
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  4 passed (4)   ← new test for silent preset passes

$ pnpm test ui/src/ui/views/
 Test Files  29 passed (29)
      Tests  297 passed (297)   ← all view tests pass, no regression
```

**Observed result after the fix**: The "Silent" preset now maps to `sessionTarget: "isolated"` which the server accepts with `payloadKind: "agentTurn"`. All 297 view tests pass with no regression.

**What was not tested**: Full Gateway integration test (the fix is a client-side mapping change; server validation already covers the isolated + agentTurn combination for all existing presets).

## Tests and validation

### Unit tests

```
$ pnpm test ui/src/ui/views/cron-quick-create.node.test.ts
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  4 passed (4)
   Start at  06:13:24
   Duration  418ms

$ pnpm test ui/src/ui/views/
 Test Files  29 passed (29)
      Tests  297 passed (297)
   Start at  06:13:32
   Duration  17.89s
```

### Root cause

1. `draftToCronFormPatch()` in `cron-quick-create.ts` maps "silent" → `sessionTarget: "main"`, `deliveryMode: "none"`
2. The mapped form state is merged with `DEFAULT_CRON_FORM` which has `payloadKind: "agentTurn"`
3. `addCronJob()` sends the job to the server via `client.request("cron.add", job)`
4. Server `assertSupportedJobSpec()` in `jobs.ts:286` throws: `'main cron jobs require payload.kind="systemEvent"'`
5. The error is caught by `withCronBusy()` → `state.cronError` is set, `saved = false`
6. The dialog stays open because `saved` is false — no visible feedback

The fix changes line 192 from `"main"` to `"isolated"`, matching what the server accepts.

## Risk checklist

- [x] This change is backwards compatible — older cron jobs are unaffected; only the quick-create mapping for the "Silent" option changes
- [x] This change has been tested with existing configurations — all 297 view tests pass
- [ ] I have updated relevant documentation — N/A, the fix is a one-line mapping correction
- [x] Breaking changes (if any) are documented in Summary — no breaking changes

## Current review state

- [x] Self-review completed
- [ ] At least one maintainer review
- [ ] All CI checks passed
